protocols.title: Omentum gel making
protocols.category: ORGANOID cultures/Omentum gel preparations (protocols from Salo)
steps: Omentum Crushed with Liquid Nitrogen Crusher 50 g (in Red-Capped Jars)
Add 50 ml 2M urea buffer pH 7.4 to the precipitate and homogenize with ultra-turrax.
Centrifuge at 14,000 rpm (tubes with a cap) with Beckman JA-30-50 or SS-34 rotor for 20 min. Recover the supernatant.
Add about 25 ml or less of 2M urea to the centrifugation tubes and homogenize again with ultrasound.
Repeat step 4 and combine the supernatants. Centrifuge once more to properly remove the precipitate.
Place the supernatant in the dialysis bag MWCO 8000 Da (Spectra/Por 7 Dialysis Tubing, 8 kD MWCO, 24 mm Flat-width, 5 meters/roll, #132114) (bags washed with Q dH2O before use).
First dialyze with 900 ml TBS + 5 ml chloroform with stirring at + 4 ° C for 2h
Replace the bags in a clean container and dialyze a further 2x with TBS at least 2h, switch to serum-free DMEM / F-12, and dialyze overnight.
Collect gels and centrifuge for an additional 14,000 rpm for 20 min. Sterile divide into 300 µl and 500 µl aliquots and store at -20°C, which does not thaw itself.

document_index.path: ORGANOID cultures/Omentum gel preparations (protocols from Salo)/Omentum gel making.docx
document_index.name: Omentum gel making.docx
document_index.title: Omentum gel making
document_index.excerpt: Make a modification a myogel protocol, omit the NaCl wash and start directly with the addition of the urea buffer. 1. Omentum Crushed with Liquid Nitrogen Crusher 50 g (in Red-Capped Jars) 2. Add 50 ml 2M urea buffer pH 7.4 to the precipitate and homogenize with ultra-turrax. 3. Place overnight with stirring at + 4°C. 4. Centrifuge at 14,000 rpm (tubes with a cap) with Beckman JA-30-50 or SS-34 rotor for 20 min. Recover the supernatant. 5. Add about 25 ml or less of 2M urea to the centrifugation tubes and homogenize again with ultrasound. 6. Repeat step 4 and combine the supernatants. Centrifuge once more to properly remove the precipitate. 7. Place the supernatant in the dialysis bag MWCO 8000 Da (Spectra/Por 7 Dialysis Tubing, 8 kD MWCO, 24 mm Flat-width, 5 meters/roll, #132114) (bags wa

Gently suspend the cells with STEM-CELLBANKER ® cryopreservation medium (1 ml for 5 x 10 5 - 5 x 10 6 5. Dispense the cell suspension in 1ml aliquots to cry opreservation vials that have been labeled with the cell line name, cell concentration, passage date an d other essential information. 6. A9165-100G, Sigma Prepare stock solution 100mM: dissolve 1.6g of the powder in 100 mL MQ water. Once dissolved, filter through 0.2 µm filter, into 2x 50 ml tube. Store in the fridge. Working solution 1mM: Add 1ml of 100mM in 99 ml of medium 2). Nicotinamide Cat no. N0636-500G, Sigma Prepare stock solution 100mM: dissolve 6,87 g of the powder in 100 mL MQ water. Once dissolved, filter through 0.2 µm filter, into 2x 50 ml tube. Store in the fridge. Working solution 5mM: Add 5ml of 100mM in 95 ml of medium 3). Y-27632 dihydrochloride Cat no. M1817 AbMoleBiosciences Prepare stock solution 5mM: dissolve 10mg (whole bottle) of the powder in 8,086 mL DMSO. No need to filter this.

Since the submission, we have already generated a significant amount of new data supporting the findings of the manuscript. If allowed for 3 months time to revise, we will be able to add even more data and address all the comments by the reviewers, which in general were in favour of publishing our work. document_index.excerpt: Heterogeneity and treatment landscape of ovarian carcinoma Notes: Solid introduction to the heterogeneity observed in HGSC, and discussion of treatments The drug efficacy testing in 3D cultures platform identifies effective drugs for ovarian cancer patients Note: Everyone agreed that this paper was well written and easy to follow. We discussed designing models specific to the questions we aim to investigate. The functional precision medicine platform described in this paper is a short-term patient derived culture very similar to the iPDCs established in our lab. document_index.title: Optimization of the HGSOC Tumor Tissue Dissociation Protocol
document_index.excerpt: Optimization of the HGSOC Tumor Tissue Dissociation Protocol Aim: To optimize the protocol for HGSOC tissue dissociation to obtain most viable tumor and immune cell populations. Background: Tissue dissociation protocol vary depending on the stiffness, cellularity and nature of the tissue type. Major factors affecting cell viability, stress response and cellular compositions are nature of the tissue dissociating enzymes, temperature and the incubation time (Flanagan et al., 2019, Adam et al., 2017). In addition, buffers and media used in the protocol might also have an effect on the cell viability and the composition. Despite the findings are well presented, this study did not provide sufficient intellectual advances in this topic and I do not support it to be published in Science Advances. 1. document_index.title: Reviewer comments and point-by-point responses
document_index.excerpt: Reviewer comments and point-by-point responses We thank the reviewers for their important insights on our work. We have now revised the manuscript and we have addressed all the comments, including an extensive restructuring of the figures, inclusion of supplemental data to the main figures, and edited numerous sections of the main text to improve the presentation of the data and to clarify the messages conveyed by our work. The major changes to the work are: Restructuring of the figures to greatly improve the display of main figures, and improved formatting of figures for better data visibility. Inclusion of several panels of supplemental data into main figures to clarify presentation of results. Inclusion of supporting information from prior murine model studies, and clinical trial data,

document_index.title: SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIALS AND METHODS
document_index.excerpt: SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIALS AND METHODS WES data processing and analysis Bioinformatic processing of DNA samples involved several steps, including quality control, alignment to the reference human genome, deduplication, cross-sample contamination estimation, and variant discovery. Sequenced DNA reads underwent quality control and trimming steps using FastQC (1) and Trimmomatic tools (2). Subsequently, the high-quality reads were aligned to the reference human genome GRCh38.d1.vd1 using BWA-MEM (3) with default parameters, subjected to deduplication with Picard tool and base quality recalibration using the Genome Analysis Toolkit (GATK) version 4.1.9.0 (4). Cross-sample contamination estimation was conducted using GATK 4.1.9.0, with a contamination estimation threshold established at 15%. Somat

document_index.title: Karen Honey
document_index.excerpt: Cancer Immunology Research I am pleased to share a revised version of our Manuscript titled “Ex Vivo Immuno-Oncology Platform Reveals Spatial T Cell Infiltration Patterns Linked to ATR Inhibition Responses in High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer”. We greatly appreciate your time in handling our manuscript. We have addressed all of the reviewer comments during our revision of the manuscript, and hope that the manuscript can be now reconsidered for publication in Cancer Immunology Research. Anniina Färkkilä, MD PhD, Assistant Professor in Translational Gynecologic Oncology Specialist in Obstetrics and Gynecology Research Program in Systems Oncology & Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology University of Helsinki and Helsinki University Hospital 00014 University of Helsinki anniina.farkkila@helsi

document_index.title: Reviewer comments and point-by-point responses
document_index.excerpt: Reviewer comments and point-by-point responses We thank the reviewers for their important insights on our work. We have now revised the manuscript and we have addressed all the comments, including an extensive restructuring of the figures, inclusion of supplemental data to the main figures, and edited numerous sections of the main text to improve the presentation of the data and to clarify the messages conveyed by our work. The major changes to the work are: Restructuring of the figures to greatly improve the display of main figures, and improved formatting of figures for better data visibility. Inclusion of several panels of supplemental data into main figures to clarify presentation of results. Inclusion of supporting information from prior murine model studies, and clinical trial data,

document_index.title: SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIALS AND METHODS
document_index.excerpt: SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIALS AND METHODS WES data processing and analysis Bioinformatic processing of DNA samples involved several steps, including quality control, alignment to the reference human genome, deduplication, cross-sample contamination estimation, and variant discovery. Sequenced DNA reads underwent quality control and trimming steps using FastQC (1) and Trimmomatic tools (2). Subsequently, the high-quality reads were aligned to the reference human genome GRCh38.d1.vd1 using BWA-MEM (3) with default parameters, subjected to deduplication with Picard tool and base quality recalibration using the Genome Analysis Toolkit (GATK) version 4.1.9.0 (4). Cross-sample contamination estimation was conducted using GATK 4.1.9.0, with a contamination estimation threshold established at 15%. Somat

document_index.title: Senior Editor Comments:
document_index.excerpt: The novel human ex vivo experimental system presented is timely and of interest. Results provide proof-of-principle for the potential utility of this system to investigate combinations of targeted agents with immunotherapy. Please, modify some of the the language in the abstract to emphasize the preliminary nature of the identified new combination treatment for HGSC, which requires further validation. In addition, all issues raised by the reviewers should be addressed, and some data moved from supplementary figures to main figures. Goals:
Functional testing of novel immunotherapy combinations in iPDCs, and evaluate the utility of iPDCs for precision oncology approaches
Investigate baseline ATR and immune pathway activation as a predictor for sensitivity to combinatorial ATR inhibition on iPDC matched tissue samples using tCyCIF
Responsible:
Project manager : Ashwini Nagaraj 
María - Role/tasks:
Aditi - Role/tasks: master's student - optimize data analysis workflow, t-CyCIF and image analysis
Abhilash - Role/tasks:

https://github.com/AditiSirsikar/HGSC_Organoid_Drug_Plotting_Script

## Slide 1
Patient-derived immunocompetent cultures reveal personalized immunotherapies and new treatment options for therapy resistant patients with high-grade serous ovarian cancer
Anniina, Färkkilä, MD PhD 
Assistant Professor, Specialist in Gynecology
University of Helsinki and Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland

## Slide 2
Disclosure InformationOvarian CancerOctober 5-7, 2023 | Westin Seaport Boston | Boston, MA
Anniina Färkkilä
I have the following relevant financial relationships to disclose:
 Consultant for: GSK
 Speaker’s Bureau for: GSK, Astra Zeneca
 Grant/Research support from: GSK, Astra Zeneca

## Slide 3
High-Grade Serous Ovarian Carcinoma (HGSC)
Most common (~70%) and lethal subtype of ovarian cancers
 ~ 80% of the patients exhibit omental metastasis
HGSC harbor genotype-specific tumor microenvironment (TME)
BRCA1/2 mut tumors exhibit increased immunosurveillance compared to HR wt
Enhanced spatial interactions between proliferating epithelial cells and T cells in BRCA1/2 mut tumors (Launonen et al., 2022) 
Treatment responses
PARPi effective against HRD tumors
Immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) response rate is 8-10% (Zhu et al., 2021)
Better response to combination of PARPi plus anti-PD-1 in HRD patients (Färkkilä et al., 2020)

## Slide 14
DO NOT POST
DO NOT POST
Summary
Novel patient-derived OmGel matrix significantly improves the growth of HGSC patient-derived cultures compared to conventional matrix

iPDCs maintain source tumor-derived lymphocytes and myeloid cells critical for eliciting response to immunotherapy combinations 

iPDCs exhibit tumor genotype-specific response to Olaparib and anti-PD1 treatments, and recapitulate clinical response to PARPi or chemotherapy

 The presence of physiologically relevant patient-derived matrix combined with patient-specific tumor infiltrating immune cells makes iPDCs a reliable pre-clinical model for high throughput testing of novel immuno-oncology drugs and combination therapies

Question answered by Jie
Why are we using the Integrated Intensity for CK7 and Mean Intensity for CD45?
To distinguish between the intensity of these two cell populations the integrated intensity, which accounts for the size of the cell, is recorded for the tumor cells. Since tumor cells are larger the intensity values for the tumor cells are also greater allowing for gating by intensity
Most of the double positives in S141 were dead and the double positives cells (CK7+ and CD45+) were not visible in the images. How do we approach categorizing these cells?
Since it is not clear whether the CK7 or CD45 cells have become apoptotic, we have decided to include double positives in the all cell count, but we will categorize them as double negatives

Hello Pirkko, 
I’m Aditi Sirsikar, a master’s student in Anniina Farkkila’s lab. We have HGSC patient-derived immunocompetent cultures in a humanized matrix. We would like to run scRNA sequencing on these samples. 
Describe how the samples have been stored. These are the control samples, they are not treated with any drugs only DMSO 2 and the purpose of the experiments is to evaluate whether the cultured sample recapitulates main gene expression signatures as the original (ex. Vivo = out of the living) parental tumor and to tease out pathways up and down regulated in the tumor facilitating growth of the tumor to provide an explanation for the drug responses plotted earlier. 
We have a few questions about potential interference from foreign rna from the gel matrix derived from human tissue (pooled omentum tissue from multiple patient samples)
Another question: how do you recommend separating the clumps of cells (mechanically using a strainer?)
----Decided that we will run a short cycif experiment instead of single cell sequencing

## Slide 20
Methods (Raw images to Cell Profiler):
384 well plate (markers DCM nuclei staining, CK7 cell staining CD45 cell staining) → Imaging with Opera scanner (scanning takes 10-15 images of well and the microscope focuses on area in the well that have clumps of cells or spheroids)→ cell profiler (explanation of what cell profiler does; quantification of intensity values) → intensity values are outputted per cell (so each row of the csv file is per cell and information about the well and image number the cell is from are listed which we use later to group the cells by image and treatment) → using imageJ the intensity thresholds for DCM, CK7, and CD45 per patient

## Slide 21
Methods (Cell Profiler to Script)

LITERATURE REVIEW FOR ATXi (GLPG1690)/PERKi (AMG PERK 44) OPTIMIZATION PLAN
Matilda Salko 07/2021
Elina – tästä meitä kiinnostavasta GLPG1690:sta ei löytynyt juurikaan in vitro -tutkimuksia PubMedistä. PERKi-inhibiittori AMG PERK 44:n suhteen vielä heikommat löydökset (haasteena on tietysti saattanut olla myös etsijän tiedonhakutaidot…). Tässä alussa on vähän muistiinpanoja taustasta ja LPA-pathwaysta ylipäätään, lähinnä mulle itselleni opetukseksi. Alempana ne edes suhteellisen relevantilta vaikuttavat artikkelit, joita löysin. 
Information from Juan R Cubillos-Ruiz: “These are the conditions that we would suggest testing in your system to determine if they are able to modulate (hopefully improve) response to IO + PARPi:
Autotaxin inhibitor GLPG1690 used at final concentrations ranging from 0.2 - 1 uM
PERK inhibitor AMG PERK 44 used at final concentrations ranging from 0.2 - 1 uM
Combination of 1 and 2. above “
LPA (lysophosphatidic acid) in tumors
 PMID: 33526777 (review 2021):
LPA is a water-soluble phospholipid, which, by current knowledge, acts through 6 g protein-coupled receptors: LPAR1-LPAR6.
In addition GPR87 (g protein-coupled receptor 87) and (orphan) P2Y10
TRPV1 (transient receptor potential vanilloid 1) responds to LPA stimuli, in addition to other protein-mediated stimuli.
Produced during cell membrane synthesis and acts as a robust extracellular signaling molecule present in tissues and blood plasma. 
LPA-mediated signaling is responsible for proliferation, migration, and cytoskeletal reorganization, to name a few. 
LPA is present in all eukaryotic tissues and fluids, both in physiological and pathological conditions
LPA can be present both intra- and extracellularly

vector_chunks.chunk_id: ORGANOID cultures/Literature review for ATXi-PERKi optimization plan.docx::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: ORGANOID cultures/Literature review for ATXi-PERKi optimization plan.docx
vector_chunks.text:
g is responsible for proliferation, migration, and cytoskeletal reorganization, to name a few. 
LPA is present in all eukaryotic tissues and fluids, both in physiological and pathological conditions
LPA can be present both intra- and extracellularly
One major pathway involved in LPA extracellular synthesis, where lysophospholipids (LPs, which are produced from membrane phospholipid precursors), are converted into LPA by autotaxin (ATX; ENPP2).
In (solid) cancers, increased LPA signaling is related to numerous roles:
LPAR1 and LPAR4 - mostly associated with cell motility and invasion
LPAR4 is strongly expressed in ovaries under physiological conditions
LPAR2 - cell survival and apoptosis inhibition
LPAR3 and LPAR6 - promotion of tumor proliferation, migration, invasiveness, aggressiveness, and progression of different cancers
Also, LPAR3 has been related to the regulation of dendritic cell migration, and possibly having an antitumor immunity role
LPAR5 - involved in immune response inhibition and cancer progression
ATX and LPA are released by both tumor cells and other cells in the TME, increasing invasion, suppressing antitumor response and contributing to therapy resistance.
LPA-LPAR signaling has also been demonstrated to promote EMT
LPA has also been shown to induce the production of proinflammatory cytokines, such as IL6 and IL8 - impacting the progression and metastasis. An inflammatory environment creates a positive loop where LPA-LPAR signaling is enhanced
Increased LPA signaling has been linked to carcinogenesis and tumor progression as well as reprogramming of metastatic microenvironments and development of therapy resistance
One study taken into account in this review reported that chemotherapy inhibits expression of LPAR1-3

vector_chunks.text:
been linked to carcinogenesis and tumor progression as well as reprogramming of metastatic microenvironments and development of therapy resistance
One study taken into account in this review reported that chemotherapy inhibits expression of LPAR1-3
In addition, there is a positive loop in which the inflammatory milieu induces more LPA-LPAR signaling and chronic inflammation can lead to malignant transformation.
LPA receptors and signaling intermediates. LPA signals through six different LPA transmembrane G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs and LPAR1–6) and other GPCRs (such as P2Y10 and GRP87). These receptors activate different intracellular signaling mediators to elicit different/several cellular responses (such as cytoskeleton reorganization, migration, proliferation, survival, and cell–cell communication). Finally, LPA was also recently described as a direct PPARγ agonist and in this sense intracellular LPA could also induce signaling through this receptor. Scheme created using Biorender.com
In ovarian cancer:
“LPA has a tremendously described influence on ovarian cancer. It acts on the EMT, glucose metabolism, cell migration, among other roles; and also functions as a biomarker.” (ref PMID: 33526777). 
In ovarian cancer, LPA affects the EMT, glucose metabolism, cell migration, and other factors
LPA-promoted glycolytic shift could lead to transformation of normal fibroblasts to CAFs
LPA Induces Metabolic Reprogramming in Ovarian Cancer via a Pseudohypoxic Response (2018, PMID: 29386184)
Study used patient-derived ovarian cancer cells (isolated from ascites samples), and HGSOC cell lines (ex. Kuramochi, OVCAR8, SKOV3-ip among others) to demonstrate the effect of LPA signaling on metabolism of OC cells

ATX function Zhang X, Li M, Yin N, Zhang J. The Expression Regulation and Biological Function of Autotaxin. Cells. 2021 Apr 19;10(4):939. doi: 10.3390/cells10040939. PMID: 33921676; PMCID: PMC8073485. (review)
ATX-LPA-LPA receptor signaling axis and LPA delivery on the surface of target cell. LPA is mainly synthesized via the ATX-mediated hydrolysis of lysophosphatidylcholine (LPC) and degraded to monoacylglycerol (MAG) through LPP1/3. ATX protein is composed of two N-terminal somatomedin B (SMB)-like domains, a central phosphodiesterase (PDE) domain, and a C-terminal nuclease (NUC)-like domain. The SMB2 domain enables ATX to bind with cell surface integrin, which achieves a highly efficient delivery of LPA to its receptors. When it binds to LPAR1-6, LPA induces different downstream signal cascades, including Rho, phospholipase C (PLC), mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK), phosphatidylinositide 3-kinase (PI3K), and adenylate cyclase (AC), through different G proteins.
The mechanism of ATX expression in cancer cells still not fully understood

In IPF, collagen is excessively deposited in the lungs

ATX inhibitor GLPG1690 was combined with irradiation
GLPG1690 is currently in phase III trials for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis
The mice received 50 or 100 mg/kg of GLPG1690
Combining GLPG1690 with irradiation did not decrease tumor size significantly more compared to irradiation only
However the amount of Ki67 positive cells did decrease, among other effects
This was associated with increased levels of cleaved caspase-3 and decreased levels of Bcl-2
In combination with doxorubicin (chemo), GLPG1690 synergistically inhibited tumor growth

Sulata kudosta jääkaapissa niin, että voit paloitella sen pieniksi palasiksi (alle 1 cm ∙ 1 cm ∙ 1 cm). 
Laita vetokaappiin max-poisto ja huoneen väliovi auki.

Laite käynnistetään start-napista ja murskainta käytetään kudoksesta riippuen 1-3 min. (Myooma n. 3 min, omentum ja lymfo n. 1 min). Lisää aikaa plus-näppäimestä.
Kammio irrotetaan laitteesta, kopistetaan jauhe kannesta ja avataan varovasti. Nosta kuula pois pinseteillä.
Käytä lämpöhansikkaita sillä kammio on jäätävän kylmä. 
Kudosmurska otetaan talteen nestetypellä kylmennetyllä lusikalla joko suoraan sentrifuugiputkeen tai sopivaan näytepurkkiin, jos valmistusta ei jatketa heti.

Kun viet tankin täytettäväksi, päästä paine pois 3-hanasta ja avaa letkuosan ”kaulus”. Ota tiiviste erilleen hyllylle. Kun kytket letkut takaisin, pidä kolmoshana auki.

Ohje on 10 grammalle kudosmurskaa, mutta ohjetta voi skaalata tarpeen mukaan. Punnitse paljonko murskaa on.
Muista steriilit välineet! Muista laittaa lattiafuugi jäähtymään kaikkina päivinä.

os sentrifuugiputkeen ja fuugaa 14 000 rpm 20 min +4oC. Yhdistä supernatantit.
Sentifugoi yhdistetyt supernatantit 14000 rpm 20 min +4 oC. 
Huuhtele dialyysipussit (MWCO 8 kDa) steriilillä vedellä sentrifugoinnin aikana noin 30 min ennen käyttöä. (n. 35 cm on riittävä 12 ml:lle liuosta) Tee pussiin solmu ja laita sen yläpuolelle klipsi.
Pipetoi supernatantti puhtaaseen falconiin ja sieltä dialyysipussiin ja sulje pussi klipsillä. Jätä tarpeeksi tyhjää tilaa, kun täytät pussin.
Laita dialyysipussit mittalasiin, jossa sekoitettu 1000 ml TBS + 5,6 ml kloroformia. Anna sekoittua magneettisekoittajalla +4 oC:ssa ainakin 2 h. Jos on suurempi erä käytä 2 l TBS:ää ja/tai vaihda 1 h välein.
Laita uuteen mittalasiin 1 l TBS:ää ja siirrä dialyysipussit tähän. Anna sekoittua +4 oC:ssa ainakin kaksi tuntia.
Vaihda uusi TBS ja anna sekoittua +4 oC:ssa ainakin 2 h.
Laita uuteen mittalasiin 1 litra seerumitonta mediumia (DMEM/F-12). Siirrä dialyysipussit tähän. Anna olla sekoituksessa yön yli +4 oC:ssa.
(päivä 3 tai 4)
Siirrä geeli sentrifuugiputkeen, sentrifugoi 14 000 rpm 20 min +4 oC ja kerää geelisuppi talteen puhtaaseen Falconiin. 
Ota proteiinin mittausta varten 50 µl syrjään ja jaa geeli sopiviin eriin (esimerkiksi 300 µl – 10 ml) steriilisti laminaarissa. Säilytä geelit-20 oC:ssa.
Tee proteiininmääritys 1:2, 1:5 ja 1:10 laimennoksesta.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: ORGANOID cultures/Omentum gel preparations (protocols from Salo)/Omentum gel making.docx::chunk_0000
vector_chunks.source_file: ORGANOID cultures/Omentum gel preparations (protocols from Salo)/Omentum gel making.docx
vector_chunks.text:
Omentum gel making
Make a modification a myogel protocol, omit the NaCl wash and start directly with the addition of the urea buffer.
1. Omentum Crushed with Liquid Nitrogen Crusher 50 g (in Red-Capped Jars)
2. Add 50 ml 2M urea buffer pH 7.4 to the precipitate and homogenize with ultra-turrax.
3. Place overnight with stirring at + 4°C.
4. Centrifuge at 14,000 rpm (tubes with a cap) with Beckman JA-30-50 or SS-34 rotor for 20 min. Recover the supernatant.
5. Add about 25 ml or less of 2M urea to the centrifugation tubes and homogenize again with ultrasound.
6. Repeat step 4 and combine the supernatants. Centrifuge once more to properly remove the precipitate.
7. Place the supernatant in the dialysis bag MWCO 8000 Da (Spectra/Por 7 Dialysis Tubing, 8 kD MWCO, 24 mm Flat-width, 5 meters/roll, #132114) (bags washed with Q dH2O before use).
8. First dialyze with 900 ml TBS + 5 ml chloroform with stirring at + 4 ° C for 2h
9. Replace the bags in a clean container and dialyze a further 2x with TBS at least 2h, switch to serum-free DMEM / F-12, and dialyze overnight.
10. Collect gels and centrifuge for an additional 14,000 rpm for 20 min. Sterile divide into 300 µl and 500 µl aliquots and store at -20°C, which does not thaw itself.

## Slide 1
Färkkilä-GSK collaboration on STING agonist GSK3859856

Project update
12.09.2023

## Slide 2
High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC)
Most common (~70%) and lethal subtype of ovarian cancers
 ~ 80% of the patients exhibit omental metastasis


Frequent genetic alterations
homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) (~50%)
mutation in TPS3, BRCA1/2 genes (~20%)


Potential targeted therapies
PARPi effective against HRD tumors
immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICI) response rate is 8-10%
better response to combination of PARPi plus anti-PD-1 in HRD HGSC
Nieman K.M et al., 2011
Cojocaru et al., 2017
Färkkilä et al., 2020

## Slide 3
Research objective:
To test STING agonist (GSK3859856) alone and in combinations with anti- PD-1 antibody, PARP inhibitor and other agents on patient-derived immunocompetent cultures (iPDCs) established from HGSC patient’s undergoing surgery at Helsinki University Hospital.
Readouts:
 a) Cytotoxic response from tumor and immune cells using image-based analysis 
 b) Abundance and functional states of immune cells using multiparameter flow cytometry

## Slide 4
Novel patient-derived matrix improves the growth of HGSC patient-derived cultures while preserving the histology and genomic features of the source tumors

## Slide 5
Source tumor-derived immune cells are preserved in the OmGel cultures
iPDCs

iPDCs show HRD genotype-specific response to PARPi and anti-PD1 treatments

Results from the plots did not accurately represent abundance of cells in images (Ashwini re-set thresholds for each sample and created a spreadsheet with thresholds per patient → automatically loaded into script)

Handling Na values and Inf (I went through each sample to determine the reason for NaNa or Inf values. Calculating ratio produced NA values if there are 0 cells, of a certain criteria, in the treatment group wells. Inf values are produced if the DMSO group wells contain 0 cells. For this reason, in the heatmaps I set Na values to the lowest fold change value and Inf to the highest fold change value so that this data can still be plotted)

Which ratios are the most meaningful? (Review dot heatmaps together)

Tried clustering the samples (but the samples did not cluster by HDR status, perhaps we need more samples for the clustering to be informative)

Looks like many cells died in the DMSO wells in sample S141, S147, and both S156 samples (Unfortunately these samples are mostly HRD. Perhaps we can divide or subtract by the DMSO proportion?)

Next steps
Include 2-3 more samples for 384 plate combination test?

Select 1 & 7 uM ATRi in combination with 0.2 & 0.7 uM ATXi for functional activation of T cells (Abhilash). More combinations to be tested for activation of T cells?

For the drug combination-treated samples, select d0 samples for scRNA seq (Aditi)?

## Page 1
 
 
 
 
Cell Freezing: 
For optimum results, cells for cryopreservation sho uld be in log phase of growth. Similar or standard 
freezing protocols may be substituted. 
1. Examine and make sure the cell culture is free of c ontamination, in healthy situation and proper 
confluency, etc. 
2. Perform a cell count to determine the viability of cells 
3. Gently pellet the cells by centrifugation (3 - 5 mi nutes at 1,000~2,000rpm, 4 oC). Remove the 
supernatant by using an aspirator. 
4. Gently suspend the cells with STEM-CELLBANKER ® cryopreservation medium (1 ml for 5 x 10 5 - 5 x 10 6 
cells). 
5. Dispense the cell suspension in 1ml aliquots to cry opreservation vials that have been labeled with the 
cell line name, cell concentration, passage date an d other essential information. 
6. Place the vials directly in a -80 oC for storage. If necessary, transfer the frozen vi als to a liquid nitrogen 
storage tank after the vials have been frozen for a t least 24 hours. 
7. Optimum protocol may change with the cell types. 
 
 
 
 
 STEM -CELLBANKER ® DMSO 
FREE 
Cryopreservation Medium 
• Serum-Free 
• Chemically Defined 
• GMP Manufactured 
 
 
Cat#: 11897F 
Qty: 20ml 
Expiry Date: 3 years from manufacturing date (see l abel) 
 

## Page 2
 
 
 
Thawing: 
1. Remove the frozen cell from storage and quickly tha w in a 37 oC shaking water bath. 
2. Immediately dilute and gently mix each 1ml of cells with 10ml of complete cell culture medium. 
3. Gently pellet the cells by centrifugation (3-5 minu tes at 1,000 - 2,000rpm, 4 oC). Remove the supernatant 
by aspirator. 
4. Gently suspend the cells with appropriate volume of complete cell culture medium and plate in a cultur e 
flask. 
5. Continue the further culture procedures according t o standard protocols.

by aspirator. 
4. Gently suspend the cells with appropriate volume of complete cell culture medium and plate in a cultur e

5. Continue the further culture procedures according t o standard protocols. 
Guarantee of Quality: 
1. Manufactured in compliance with JPN, EU, US, and PI C/S GMP guidelines 
2. Bacterial contamination free – Product has been tes ted and confirmed to be free of bacteria, fungi and 
mycoplasma. 
3. Chemical Analysis: pH (7.0 to 8.5 at room temperatu re) Endotoxin (below <5 EU/mL) 
4. Performance test – Cell viability above 80% (JM404, SK-007) is guaranteed. 
Storage of STEM-CELLBANKER ®: 
1. STEM-CELLBANKER ® should be stored at 4 oC or below. 
2. Keep frozen in case of long term unused period (3 m onths or longer) 
3. Repeated freezing and thawing may impair the qualit y of the product, it is recommended that STEM-
CELLBANKER ® is aliquoted before freezing.

Prepare all the stock solutions inside the laminar and keep the final solution sterile
1). N-Acetyl-L-cysteine Cat no. A9165-100G, Sigma
Prepare stock solution 100mM: dissolve 1.6g of the powder in 100 mL MQ water. Once dissolved, filter through 0.2 µm filter, into 2x 50 ml tube. Store in the fridge.
Working solution 1mM: Add 1ml of 100mM in 99 ml of medium
2). Nicotinamide Cat no. N0636-500G, Sigma
Prepare stock solution 100mM: dissolve 6,87 g of the powder in 100 mL MQ water. Once dissolved, filter through 0.2 µm filter, into 2x 50 ml tube. Store in the fridge.
Working solution 5mM: Add 5ml of 100mM in 95 ml of medium
3). Y-27632 dihydrochloride Cat no. M1817 AbMoleBiosciences
Prepare stock solution 5mM: dissolve 10mg (whole bottle) of the powder in 8,086 mL DMSO. No need to filter this. Aliquot 500 uL per eppendorf. Store at -20.
Working solution 5uM: Add 100ul of 5mM in 100 ml of medium
4). Recombinant human FGF10 Cat no.100-26 Peprotech
Prepare stock solution 100ug/ml: Dissolve 25 µg in 250 µL sterile 0.1% BSA in DPBS (without Ca & Mg). Do not filter it aliquot into 50 ul per Eppendorf. Store at -20.
Working solution 10 ng/ml: Add 10ul of 100ug/ml in 100 ml of medium
5). Animal-Free Recombinant Human FGF-basic (154 a.a.) Cat no. AF-100-18B 
Prepare stock solution 100ug/ml: Dissolve 50 µg (supplied) in 500 µL sterile 0.1% BSA in DPBS (without Ca & Mg). Do not filter it aliquot into 50 ul per Eppendorf. Store at -20.
Working solution 10 ng/ml: Add 10ul of 100ug/ml in 100 ml of medium
6). Animal-Free Recombinant Human EGF Cat no. AF-100-15 Peprotech 
Prepare stock solution 100mg/ml: Dissolve 100 mg (1 mg supplied) in 1mL sterile 0.1% BSA in DPBS (without Ca & Mg). Do not filter it aliquot into 100 ul per Eppendorf. Store at -20.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx::chunk_0000
vector_chunks.source_file: ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text:
Appeal to the editor in Science Translational Medicine
We thank the reviewers for their overall positive comments. They find our work intriguing and and promising, and they appreciate our detailed analyses of difficult-to-get samples. 
The main criticisms were mostly on the small sample numbers and correlations to clinical data. Since the submission, we have already generated a significant amount of new data supporting the findings of the manuscript. If allowed for 3 months time to revise, we will be able to add even more data and address all the comments by the reviewers, which in general were in favour of publishing our work. 
The main improvements will be
1) providing the clinical outcome correlations in XX patients 
2) addition of XX patients for the tCycIF TME and biomarker analysis
3) detailed single-cell analysis of immune activation after the treatments
These additional data will be comprehensively address all comments from the reviewers and significantly strengthen the conclusions of the study.
Below please find the point-by-point rebuttal letter addressing all the Reviewer’s comments. 
Reviewer: 1
This manuscript describes a novel approach to high throughput in vitro drug screening in patient-derived organoids from women with ovarian cancer. The authors describe advantages to this approach including the ability to evaluate treatment effects on both tumor and immune cell populations, and the ability to assess the efficacy of immune therapy combination regimens. They state that prior organoid models were assessed for immune cell function but here the focus was on cytotoxic effects and tumor intrinsic biomarkers of response.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text:
tions, and the ability to assess the efficacy of immune therapy combination regimens. They state that prior organoid models were assessed for immune cell function but here the focus was on cytotoxic effects and tumor intrinsic biomarkers of response. Strengths of the manuscript include the attention to potential confounding effects of culture conditions with the observation that tissue dissociation methods could induce a stress response associated with chemoresistance, the analysis of tumor samples collected at secondary debulking to evaluate treatment resistant samples, and the quantification of treatment effects on immune cells as a potential toxicity to consider in establishing dosing. Weaknesses of this report are primarily related to the small sample sizes and the lack of clinical correlation for validation in most cases. Because of this, some of the conclusions are overstated - such as the assertion that this model system can stratify patients into likely responders and nonresponders to treatment. This approach requires substantial clinical validation prior to advancing the model as a strategy to assign patients to treatment regimens.
We thank the reviewer for constructive feedback on our manuscript and highlighting the strengths of this study.
Specific issues that should be addressed by the authors prior to publication include:
There is clinical correlation for PARPi treatment efficacy in samples from PDS available for only one patient. The association between HRD and PARPi sensitivity in vitro is well established, but results in patients are more complex, particularly with evidence that PARPi have immunomodulatory effects.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text:
fficacy in samples from PDS available for only one patient. The association between HRD and PARPi sensitivity in vitro is well established, but results in patients are more complex, particularly with evidence that PARPi have immunomodulatory effects. It is unclear whether the inclusion of an immune component in the iPDC can improve prediction beyond tumor specific in vitro cultures without a comparison with patient data for validation.
We acknowledge that the numbers were small in the original manuscript. We have now collected clinical data for PARPi and or platinum sensitivity from 20 patients which will be correlated with responses from corresponding iPDCs. Given 3 months time for the revisions, we can provide analyses of up to XX samples significantly strengthening the conclusions of the manuscript. 
While it is true that the pro and anti-tumor immunomodulatory effects of PARPi have been established (REF) through migration and activation of immune cells and by impairing DNA damage in immune cells and upregulation of PD-L1 on tumor cells. We focused on validating the iPDC model by evaluating the effect of PARPi on cancer cells to clinically validated / predicted HRD-specific anti-tumor response. However, we have now also performed flow cytometry analysis from PARPi treated iPDCs. From this data we can analyze activation states of T cells, Dendritic cells, and NK cells to evaluate immune cell-specific responses to PARPi. We can provide this result during the revision stage along with the clinical correlation to PARPi sensitivity. 
While the authors are to be commended for investigating samples from patients with recurrent disease after treatment, they only had samples from 3 people with markedly different treatment histories.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx::chunk_0003
vector_chunks.source_file: ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ith the clinical correlation to PARPi sensitivity. 
While the authors are to be commended for investigating samples from patients with recurrent disease after treatment, they only had samples from 3 people with markedly different treatment histories. While two are described as having progressed on PARPi and chemo, it’s not clear if they would still be considered platinum sensitive based on the diagram in Fig 3A or platinum resistant. Response to PARPi correlates with platinum sensitivity so this would be helpful information to have. Additionally, there is no description of what chemo regimens they received or why they underwent secondary debulking-which is relatively uncommon after 2-3 recurrences, particularly in the setting of disease resistant to multiple chemo regimens. Furthermore, it appears that the investigators do not have data from pre-treatment samples from these same patients to enable them to identify changes associated with chemoresistance – they cite changes in estrogen, TGFb, WNT as being implicated in resistance but don’t have information about baseline expression. Finally, it does not appear that these patients received any of the tested agents with the exception of the patient who continued on olaparib, so there is no clinical correlation available to validate the translational relevance of iPDC outcomes or the associated genomic pathway analyses. Ultimately, the data in Fig 3 and the analysis of drug sensitivities in resistant tumors present detailed descriptions of three cases but are not sufficient to support the conclusion that “iPDCs enable investigation of drug sensitivity and resistance mechanisms in therapy resistant HGSC patients”.

The three recurrent samples analyzed in this manuscript are representative cases to evaluate if iPDCs can mimic clinically known drug resistance, as well as guide in the identification of effective drugs or combinations in the relapse settings. These three patients underwent secondary debulking surgeries due to oligometastatic disease, and due to no prior debulking surgery. The two patients #C218 and #C429 progressed on PARPi and chemotherapy, respectively they were considered as platinum sensitive base on the primary response to platinum-free interval (PFI) which was 24 months (#C218) and 20 months (#C429). Although they showed initial sensitivity to platinum and PARPi, later developed progressive disease. We will provide a detailed supplementary table for chemotherapy regimens and response table for these three patients. 
The pre-treatment samples are available for 2 of the patients - and we have performed WES on the pretreatment samples. During the revisions we will perform tCycIF single-cell spatial analysis of the pretreatment samples - to investigate the tumor-immune landscapes of the pre- and post treatment samples including ER, TGFb, and WNT signaling.
We acknowledge that the conclusion of “iPDCs enable investigation of drug sensitivity and resistance mechanisms in therapy resistant HGSC patients” is not sufficient based on three samples. Hence, we will tone down our statement to “iPDCs may aid in the investigation of drug sensitivity and resistance mechanisms in therapy resistant HGSC patients”.

mechanisms in therapy resistant HGSC patients” is not sufficient based on three samples. Hence, we will tone down our statement to “iPDCs may aid in the investigation of drug sensitivity and resistance mechanisms in therapy resistant HGSC patients”.
Although the emphasis here is on tumor-intrinsic factors like cytotoxicity, the innovation of the iPDC model is the ability to assess immunologic effects of therapy and the efficacy of immune therapy – tumor extrinsic effects. Because the iPDC can only be maintained for a short time, the analyses of effects on immune cells are restricted to immediate effects on pre-existing tumor infiltrating or tumor-associated lymphocytes. Other mechanisms contributing to the efficacy of immune therapy, such as activation of new T cell populations recognizing neoantigens or changes in T cell recruitment to tumor sites, would be missed in the iPDC system. Induction of immune memory also cannot be assessed.
As the reviewer pointed out, the limitation of iPDCs is that it only allows investigation of immunotherapy effects on intra-tumoral immune cells but not on the mobility and priming of the peripheral blood immune cells. Despite this limitation, the fact that iPDCs harbor patient-matched intratumor immune cells makes them a useful preclinical model to evaluate effectiveness of immuno-oncology agents in activating intra-tumoral immune cells. We will include this point in the Discussion.
Some of the immunomodulatory effects attributed to the anti-tumor agents tested may instead result from co-culture with dying tumor cells. Increased tumor cell death with release of DAMPs would be expected to enhance DC activation and subsequent T cell activation.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM.docx::chunk_0006

vector_chunks.text:
munomodulatory effects attributed to the anti-tumor agents tested may instead result from co-culture with dying tumor cells. Increased tumor cell death with release of DAMPs would be expected to enhance DC activation and subsequent T cell activation. No controls were evaluated to distinguish direct effects on leukocyte subsets from indirect effects in coculture with tumor.
We agree that dying tumor cells might cause some of the immunomodulatory effects in iPDCs and in patient tumors. Therefore, we believe that assessing the immunomodulatory effects on monocultures of leukocyte subsets may not be translationally relevant, as it does not mimic tumor and immune cell interactions observed in the native tumors. However, we always normalize the results to un-treated control to exclude culture-induced changes in tumor and immune cell functional states. This will be clarified further in the manuscript. 
In addition, leukocyte attrition resulting from time in culture was not explicitly considered. Although the authors assert that no significant changes in leukocyte distribution was observed in different iPDC preparation conditions or over time, there are marked differences in the distribution of leukocyte subsets in the iPDC compared with parental tumors in Figure 2B suggesting that engraftment in culture may have edited or enriched leukocyte populations.
While we observed heterogeneity in the immune cell proportions, these did not reach statistical significance. Moreover, it is expected that any ex vivo model is subjected to some biological change compared to the native tissue due to tissue processing and in vitro culture conditions. We will include this in the Discussion to acknowledge the changes in the abundance of immune cells present in iPDCs compared to source tumor.

Lymphocyte toxicity may reflect the proliferative status of subsets which could vary by patient and by treatment. It’s difficult to draw conclusions about these complex interactions with a simple ratio. Additionally, in patients, leukopenia is attributed to marrow suppression, which cannot be captured here.
The experiments are performed in a short time frame - whereby the effects of only direct toxicities of the drugs to the different cell populations (tumor-immune cells) in the iPDCs. In the patients, bone-marrow toxicity where the peripheral blood leukocytes are typically at the lowest point at 10 days after chemotherapy, which is beyond the experiments in the iPDCs. Therefore - our approach is focused on the tumor-specific effects of the treatments. This limitation will be further clarified in the Manuscript and Discussion.

During the revisions, we will be able to add 2-3 x times more iPDC and patients to this analysis to strengthen the data and conclusions. 
The TOPACIO trial is cited as a justification for investigations of HRD tumors. It is important to note that the trial tested combined PARPi and PD1 ICI, not PD1 ICI monotherapy, and no correlation between tumor HRD status and response was observed in patients. These data and other clinical trial results showing limited efficacy of PD1 ICI are difficult to reconcile with the comparatively high response to PD1 ICI in iPDC. The authors suggest that this may be because patients receive PD1 after prior chemo, but frontline ICI combination trials in OC have also shown limited benefit. This discrepancy highlights the need for additional translational validation of this model system prior to implementation.
In our work describing the translational work on the TOPACIO clincial trial, we demonstrated a significant correlation of HRD and PFS to the combination of PARPi + PD-1 ICI (Färkkilä et al). 
Frontline ICI combination trials in ovarian cancer are still waiting for results: currently, four large Phase III clinical trials are ongoing (ENGOT-OV43/KEYLYNK-001, DUO-O, ATHENA-COMBO, ENGOT-OV44/ FIRST) investigating the efficacy of 1.st line IO + chemo combinations, with the first results expected to be released in Q2/2025. Therefore, it is currently unclear to which patients ICI should be administered in ovarian cancer, highlighting the timeliness of our efforts in identification of patients responding to ICI. 
As the frontline ICI efficacies in HGSC are unknown, our model highlights the genotype (HRD) specific responses in the patients.

Reviewer: 2
This manuscript by Nagaraj and colleagues tested a novel PDX and organoid model to study clinical predictive effects of the model in ovarian cancer. The model is interesting, and the data are generally easy to follow, but some data as noted below are hard to understand. The applicability to broad patient populations is hard to support from these data. This work, while intriguing and promising seems better suited for a specialty journal at this stage.
The model applicability to broad patient populations. For example, tissue-specific matrices have been shown to improve predictions in lymphoid metastases (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37580662/). 
Omentum is the most frequent site of intr-abodminal metastases in cancer (https://doi.org/10.1007/s13277-016-4887-3). Therefore, the OmGel applications will be highly relevant for a broad range of common cancers frequently metastasizing to the omentum - e.g. endometrial, cervical, colorectal, gastric, pancreatic cancers. 
Details
Fig. 1 Data that on differential effects of enzymes and BME versus OmGel are good. Data that cells in the OmGel preparations are olaparib-sensitive are good, but there are too few samples to conclude that this assay predicts patient response reliably.

Appeal to the editor in Science Translational Medicine
We thank the reviewers for their overall positive comments. They find our work intriguing and promising, and they appreciate our detailed analyses of difficult-to-get samples. 
The main criticisms were mostly on the small sample numbers and correlations to clinical data. Since the submission, we have already generated a significant amount of new data supporting the findings of the manuscript. If allowed for 3 months time to revise, we will be able to add even more data and address all the comments by the reviewers, which in general were in favor of publishing our work. 
The main improvements will be:
1) providing the clinical outcome correlations up to 25-30 for patients 
2) addition of up to 10 patients for the tCycIF TME and biomarker analysis
3) detailed single-cell analysis of immune activation after the treatments
These additional data will comprehensively addres all the comments from the reviewers and significantly strengthen the conclusions of the study.
Below please find the point-by-point rebuttal letter addressing all the Reviewer’s comments. 
Reviewer: 1
This manuscript describes a novel approach to high throughput in vitro drug screening in patient-derived organoids from women with ovarian cancer. The authors describe advantages to this approach including the ability to evaluate treatment effects on both tumor and immune cell populations, and the ability to assess the efficacy of immune therapy combination regimens. They state that prior organoid models were assessed for immune cell function but here the focus was on cytotoxic effects and tumor intrinsic biomarkers of response.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM_070524.docx::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: ORGANOID cultures/iPDC manuscript submission to STM/Nagaraj et al_Point-by-Point answers to review 1_ STM_070524.docx
vector_chunks.text:
tions, and the ability to assess the efficacy of immune therapy combination regimens. They state that prior organoid models were assessed for immune cell function but here the focus was on cytotoxic effects and tumor intrinsic biomarkers of response. Strengths of the manuscript include the attention to potential confounding effects of culture conditions with the observation that tissue dissociation methods could induce a stress response associated with chemoresistance, the analysis of tumor samples collected at secondary debulking to evaluate treatment resistant samples, and the quantification of treatment effects on immune cells as a potential toxicity to consider in establishing dosing. Weaknesses of this report are primarily related to the small sample sizes and the lack of clinical correlation for validation in most cases. Because of this, some of the conclusions are overstated - such as the assertion that this model system can stratify patients into likely responders and nonresponders to treatment. This approach requires substantial clinical validation prior to advancing the model as a strategy to assign patients to treatment regimens.
We thank the reviewer for constructive feedback on our manuscript and highlighting the strengths of this study.
Specific issues that should be addressed by the authors prior to publication include:
There is clinical correlation for PARPi treatment efficacy in samples from PDS available for only one patient. The association between HRD and PARPi sensitivity in vitro is well established, but results in patients are more complex, particularly with evidence that PARPi have immunomodulatory effects.

fficacy in samples from PDS available for only one patient. We acknowledge that the numbers were small in the original manuscript. We have now collected clinical data for PARPi and or platinum sensitivity from 20 patients which will be correlated with responses from corresponding iPDCs. Given 3 months time for the revisions, we can provide analyses of up to 25-30 samples significantly strengthening the conclusions of the manuscript. 
While it is true that the pro and anti-tumor immunomodulatory effects of PARPi have been established (1) through migration and activation of immune cells and by impairing DNA damage in immune cells and upregulation of PD-L1 on tumor cells. We focused on validating the iPDC model by evaluating the effect of PARPi on cancer cells to clinically validated / predicted HRD-specific anti-tumor response. However, we have now also performed flow cytometry analysis from PARPi treated iPDCs. From this data we can analyze activation states of T cells, Dendritic cells, and NK cells to evaluate immune cell-specific responses to PARPi. We can provide this result during the revision stage along with the clinical correlation to PARPi sensitivity. 
While the authors are to be commended for investigating samples from patients with recurrent disease after treatment, they only had samples from 3 people with markedly different treatment histories.

Reviewer: 2
This manuscript by Nagaraj and colleagues tested a novel PDX and organoid model to study clinical predictive effects of the model in ovarian cancer. The model is interesting, and the data are generally easy to follow, but some data as noted below are hard to understand. The applicability to broad patient populations is hard to support from these data. This work, while intriguing and promising seems better suited for a specialty journal at this stage.
The model applicability to broad patient populations. For example, tissue-specific matrices have been shown to improve predictions in lymphoid metastases (3). 
Omentum is the most frequent site of intr-abodminal metastases in cancer (4)).

vector_chunks.text:
this stage.
The model applicability to broad patient populations. For example, tissue-specific matrices have been shown to improve predictions in lymphoid metastases (3). 
Omentum is the most frequent site of intr-abodminal metastases in cancer (4)). Therefore, the OmGel applications will be highly relevant for a broad range of common cancers frequently metastasizing to the omentum - e.g. endometrial, cervical, colorectal, gastric, pancreatic cancers. 
Details
Fig. 1 Data that on differential effects of enzymes and BME versus OmGel are good. Data that cells in the OmGel preparations are olaparib-sensitive are good, but there are too few samples to conclude that this assay predicts patient response reliably.
The conclusions of the predictions will be strengthened by the addition of up to 25-30 iPDC cultures and the correlations to clinical data.
Fig. 2 Data that immune cells are represented in various in vitro preparations are good, but the subset analyses are difficult to understand. For example, the IFNγ, GzB and Ki67 data in 2F are not really comprehensible. How were the differences in expression determined? What is the flow gating strategy?
The data in Figure 2F shows the Log2FC ín the mean intensity of functional markers in CD8+ T cells from HRD or HRP iPDCs upon Olaparib or Pembrolizumab treatment. The fold change in the mean intensity of each marker is normalized to untreated control. We will clarify this detail in the corresponding Result section of the manuscript. The flow cytometry analysis for immune cell abundance was performed using CYTO (5) from the EpCAM- cell populations as shown in Supplementary Figure 2A. We will update this Figure to also include the functional marker analysis per immune cell type. 
Fig.

flow cytometry analysis for immune cell abundance was performed using CYTO (5) from the EpCAM- cell populations as shown in Supplementary Figure 2A. We will update this Figure to also include the functional marker analysis per immune cell type. 
Fig. 3 Response data are interesting but insufficient to make claims of predicting patient responses in general populations.
The conclusions of the predictions will be strengthened by the providing up to 25-30 iPDC cultures and the correlations to clinical response data for platinum and PARPi treatments.
Fig. 4 The capacity for high throughput screens are interesting but hard to understand how predictive the data will be for general patient populations. This reviewer cannot find any reported information on OmGel.
The conclusions of the predictions will be strengthened by the providing of up to 25-30 iPDC iPDC cultures and the correlations to clinical data. Full proteomic analysis and the relevance of Omentum Gel has been published by Neilson et al (6), and this it has been cited in the manuscript “Introduction line 99” and in the “Discussion line 311”.
Minor
The writing and grammar are excellent, but could nonetheless benefit from a native English speaker.
We will have the manuscript proofread by a native English speaker.

vector_chunks.text:
Heterogeneity and treatment landscape of ovarian carcinoma
Notes: Solid introduction to the heterogeneity observed in HGSC, and discussion of treatments 
The drug efficacy testing in 3D cultures platform identifies effective drugs for ovarian cancer patients
Note: Everyone agreed that this paper was well written and easy to follow. We discussed designing models specific to the questions we aim to investigate. The functional precision medicine platform described in this paper is a short-term patient derived culture very similar to the iPDCs established in our lab. However, the fPM model focused on developing organoids composed of tumor and stromal cells while disregarding immune cells. We discussed that this a significant shortcoming given the potential synergistic effect of DNA damaging drugs (DDD) and immunotherapies. In addition, the exclusion immune cells may underestimate the cytotoxic off-target effect of DDD combinations. Moving forward in the iPDC project, we aim to add fibroblasts to our cultures to study the interplay of tumor, immune, and stromal cells in an ex vivo model. In addition, we strive to test more combination therapies at different concentrations to identify effective tumor-specific novel therapies and combination therapies.

vector_chunks.text:
Optimization of the HGSOC Tumor Tissue Dissociation Protocol
Aim: To optimize the protocol for HGSOC tissue dissociation to obtain most viable tumor and immune cell populations.
Background: Tissue dissociation protocol vary depending on the stiffness, cellularity and nature of the tissue type. Major factors affecting cell viability, stress response and cellular compositions are nature of the tissue dissociating enzymes, temperature and the incubation time (Flanagan et al., 2019, Adam et al., 2017). In addition, buffers and media used in the protocol might also have an effect on the cell viability and the composition. Kopper er al., used collagenase for ovarian cancer tissue dissociation to establish organoid cultures. However, Flanagan et al., show that compared to dissociation with cold active protease at 60C, digestion with collagenase at 370C results in activation of stress response in all the tissue types. Furthermore, cold active protease yielded enhanced capture of T cells and NK cells. Here we test different enzymes, and dissociation temperatures on the HGOC tissue and evaluate the final yield, cell viability and the cell types using immunofluorescence (IF).
Materials:
Laminar hood
Sterile surgical tools and scalpel
Formaldehyde (4% in PBS)
10cm tissue culture plates
Histology cassettes
Collagenase 
Dispase
Subtilsin A
AdDF+++ medium: DMEM/F12+ 1x Glutamax+ 10mM HEPES+ Penstrep
RQ1-RNAse free DNAse
DPBS+5mM Cacl2+125U DNase
Rho Kinase Inhibitor (ROCK: Y27632)
RBC lysis buffer
70µm cell strainers
50mL falcon tubes
1.5mL / 2mL Eppendorf tubes
Protocol:
1. Dissection:
1.1. Place the tumor tissue in a 10 cm tissue culture dish inside the laminar hood. Cut the tissue into three pieces (two small and one large piece) using a sterile scalpel and or scissors.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Patient_tissues_FRESH/Optimization_of_the_HGSOC_Tumor_Tissue_Dissociation_Protocol.docx::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: Patient_tissues_FRESH/Optimization_of_the_HGSOC_Tumor_Tissue_Dissociation_Protocol.docx
vector_chunks.text:
n tubes
1.5mL / 2mL Eppendorf tubes
Protocol:
1. Dissection:
1.1. Place the tumor tissue in a 10 cm tissue culture dish inside the laminar hood. Cut the tissue into three pieces (two small and one large piece) using a sterile scalpel and or scissors. Place one of the small piece into a pre-labelled eppendorf tube and snap freeze in liquid nitrogen for DNA/RNA isolation. Place another small piece into a pre-labelled histo cassettes and fix the tissue in 4% formaldehyde (in 1X PBS) overnight at 4 0C. Use the remaining bigger piece for enzymatic dissociation and single cell isolation.
2. Enzymatic dissociation:
2.1. In the laminar hood, place the big tumor piece in a 10 cm tissue culture dish and mince into smaller pieces using a sterile scalpel and or scissors. Add couple of drops of AdDF+++ while mincing so that tissue does not dry out.
2.2. Divide the minced tumor tissue into three tubes and perform the enzymatic digestion as follows
Tube 1: 1 mg/ml Collagenase in AdDF+++ with 5uM Y-27632, incubate at @ 370C for 1 hr while keeping the tubes on a shaker.
Tube 2: 1 U/ml Dispase in AdDF+++ with 5uM Y-27632, incubate at @ 370C for 1 hr while keeping the tubes on a shaker.
Tube 3: 10 mg/ ml Subtilsin A in DPBS+5mM Cacl2+ 5uM Y-27632 @ 60C for 30 min while keeping the tubes on a shaker.
3. Filtration and centrifugation
3.1. Pre-wet 70µm strainer with cold AdDF+++ medium +5uM Y-27632+50U DNase, or DPBS+5mM Cacl2+ 5uM Y-27632+50U DNase. Pipette the digested suspensions through the strainer into 50mL tubes and wash the strainers with 5 mL above cold medium.
3.2. Centrifuge 10 minutes @ 1000 rpm 4oC. 
3.3. Repeat the step 2.2. for the tissue fragments retained on top of the cell strainer.
4. Red blood cell lysis (ONLY if there is a visible red pellet)
4.1.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: Patient_tissues_FRESH/Optimization_of_the_HGSOC_Tumor_Tissue_Dissociation_Protocol.docx::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: Patient_tissues_FRESH/Optimization_of_the_HGSOC_Tumor_Tissue_Dissociation_Protocol.docx
vector_chunks.text:
wash the strainers with 5 mL above cold medium.
3.2. Centrifuge 10 minutes @ 1000 rpm 4oC. 
3.3. Repeat the step 2.2. for the tissue fragments retained on top of the cell strainer.
4. Red blood cell lysis (ONLY if there is a visible red pellet)
4.1. Remove supernatant and resuspend the cell pellet in 2 mL RBC lysis buffer, incubate at room temperature (RT) for 5 min. Add 10 ml AdDF+++ Centrifuge 10 minutes @ 1000 rpm 4oC. 
5. Resuspension of the cell pellet for downstream analysis and cultivation
5.1. Carefully remove the supernatant and resuspend the cell pellet in AdDF+++ medium. Perform and cell count and determine total cell yield.
5.2. Pipette 5ul of concentrated cell suspension on top of a Poly-D-Lysine hydrobromide (PLD) coated slides. Air-dry horizontally 5-10 min until cells do not move (do not over-dry!) Fix 10min @ 40C with PFA and wash with PBS. Proceed with the IF.
5.3. Determine the cell concentration required for organoid culture and proceed with the cultivation.
NOTE: Refer to a detailed protocol for PLD coating, immunofluorescence, and organoid culture.

## Page 2
 
 
2 
 ONE SENTEN CE SUMMARY 30 
Patient -derived immunocompetent cultures uncover responses to immunotherapy combinations to guide 31 
precision oncology in ovarian cancer. 32 
 33 
SHORT TITLE 34 
iPDCs for precision immuno -oncology in ovarian cancer . 35 

## Page 3
 
 
3 
 ABSTRACT 36 
Identifying effective immunotherapies requires testing in pre -clinical models that represent the patient -specific 37 
immune microenvironment. Here, we established a unique patient -derived immunocompetent culture (iPDC) 38 
model for high -grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) cultured on a novel physiologically relevant patient - 39 
derived matrix. We validate our fu nctional immuno -oncology platform using histological, genomic and single - 40 
cell analysis, and show that the iPDC responses significantly correlate with the patient’s clinical responses and 41 
progression free survival. We identify new therapeutic options associ ated with distinct tumor cells states in 42 
relapsed, therapy resistant HGSC patients.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
hat the iPDC responses significantly correlate with the patient’s clinical responses and 41 
progression free survival. We identify new therapeutic options associ ated with distinct tumor cells states in 42 
relapsed, therapy resistant HGSC patients. Using h igh-throughput drug testing followed by single cell -imaging, 43 
we discover ataxia telangiectasia and Rad3 -related inhibitor (ATRi) combined with a novel immunotherapy 44 
targeting Autotaxin (ATX) as a promising new combination treatment for HGSC. The iPDC responses to ATRi 45 
plus ATX inhibitor associate with immune cell activation and a distinct source tumor biomarker profile. In 46 
conclusion, iPDCs serve as a unique, high-throughput and functional platform for testing immunotherapy 47 
combinations and predictive biomarker discovery for precision oncology in HGSC 48 

## Page 4
 
 
4 
 INTRODUCTION 49 
High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma is the most common subtype of ovarian cancer, often diagnosed at an 50 
advanced stage with 10 -year survival of 15% (1). Nearly 80% of the HGSC metastasize to omentum, a large 51 
fat tissue covering stomach and intestine, which provides metabolic and inflammatory microenvironment for 52 
cancer and immune cells (2). The s tandard of care for HGSC includes primary debulking surgery followed by 53 
platinum -based chemotherapy . Despite the successful response to initial treatment, recurrent disease often 54 
develops within the first three years . Poly (ADP -ribose) polymerase inhibitors (PARPi) have improved patient’s 55 
outcome when used as a maintenance treatment after first -line chemotherapy, particularly in patients with 56 
homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), and or sensitivity to platinum -based chemotherapy (3). 57

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf::chunk_0003
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
(PARPi) have improved patient’s 55 
outcome when used as a maintenance treatment after first -line chemotherapy, particularly in patients with 56 
homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), and or sensitivity to platinum -based chemotherapy (3). 57 
However , 35-45% of the patients discontinue the treatment due to innate or acquired resistance to PARPi (4, 58 
5), suggesting that novel combination strategies are required to overcome resistance to PARPi . 59 
Single agent immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) have shown limited efficacy in HGSC with overall response 60 
rate of 10% (6). The l ack of success with ICIs in HGSC is likely due to a variety of immunosuppressive cells 61 
and mechanisms in the tumor microenvironment (TME) (7). Targeting immunosuppressive mechanisms 62 
operated by myeloid cells is one of the strategies to improve the efficacy of ICIs. Towards this, studies from 63 
mouse models show the importance of targeting Autot axin-Lysophosphatidic acid (LPA ) pathway or PKR -like 64 
endoplasmic -reticulum (ER) kinase (PERK) to enhance CD8+ T cell response via activating interferon 65 
signaling in dendritic cells (DCs) or by depleting myeloid -derived suppressor cells (MDSC), respectively (8, 9). 66 
To improve the therapeutic benefit of ICI s, their combination with chemotherapy, PARPi, or anti -angiogenic 67 
agents are under clinical investigation (10, 11 ). Interestingly, results from the TOPACIO Phase I/II clinical trial 68 
showed that mutational signature 3 as a surrogate of HRD status predicts sensitivity to combination of PAR Pi, 69 
niraparib and anti -PD1 antibody, pembrolizumab (12). In addition, BRCA 1/2 defective HGSC has been shown 70

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf::chunk_0004
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
I/II clinical trial 68 
showed that mutational signature 3 as a surrogate of HRD status predicts sensitivity to combination of PAR Pi, 69 
niraparib and anti -PD1 antibody, pembrolizumab (12). In addition, BRCA 1/2 defective HGSC has been shown 70 
to exhibit increased immunosurveillance with more neoantigens, tumor infiltrating lymphocytes, programmed 71 
cell death (PD -1) and its ligand (PD -L1) compared to HRP tumors (13-15). These findings highlight the 72 
investigation of novel immunotherapy combinations for patients with HGSC . Various agents inhibiting DNA 73 
damag e response (DDR i) including cell cycle checkpoint inhibitors targeting ATR or WEE1 kinases are being 74 
clinically explored (16). While preclinical studies implicate benefit of combin ing of DDR i and IC Is (17), 75 
investigation of novel combinations of ATRi with other DNA damaging agents or immunotherapies is required 76 
to guide personalized immuno -oncology in ovarian cancer. However, prior to clinical investigation of novel 77 

## Page 5
 
 
5 
 immunotherapies, the efficacy of these agents needs to be validated in immunocompetent models representing 78 
patient tumor -specific immune microenvironment . 79 
A variety of patient -derived ex vivo models have been developed to model tumor immune microenvironment, 80 
and to evaluate response to immunotherapies (18-20). All these studies have utilized matrigel or collagen - 81 
based matrices to culture the cells or tissue fragments. However, the source of these matrices being commonly 82 
of mouse or rat origin, do not represent HGSC patient TME. In addition, previous studies using patient -derived 83 
models have primarily focused on the e ffect of ICIs on functional responses from immune cells but not the 84

ces being commonly 82 
of mouse or rat origin, do not represent HGSC patient TME. In addition, previous studies using patient -derived 83 
models have primarily focused on the e ffect of ICIs on functional responses from immune cells but not the 84 
cytotoxic response from tumor cells. Furthermore, these models are not suitable for high -throughput 85 
combinatorial drug testing. Human tissue -specific extracellular matri ces (ECM) have been produced to model 86 
the primary and metastatic TME (21, 22). Towards this, Neilson et al., have generated and characterized a 87 
human omentum gel matrix (OmGel) from tumor -free omentum , which has the potential utility for studying 88 
omental metastatic microenvironment across a plethora of tumor types with frequent metastasis to oment um 89 
(23). Here we established a functional precision immuno -oncology platform using novel HGSC patient -derived 90 
immunocompetent cultures (iPDCs) using patient -derived OmGel matrix . We validated that the iPDCs 91 
preserve tumor and immune cell features of the native tumor and recapitulate the clinical responses to 92 
chemotherapy and PARPis. Importantly, by coupling single cell image -based drug responses with immune cell 93 
functional states we show that iPDCs reveal patient -specific vulnerabilities to novel immuno -oncology and 94 
combination therap ies, which associate with distinct tissue biomarkers in HGSC. 95

In this study, we have processed 4 7 HGSC tumors from 44 patients and established cultures from 3 7 tumors 99 
(Table. S, 1 ). We first started with optimizing tumor tissue dissociation , as the e nzymatic dissociation is a 100 
necessary step in setting up ex vivo cultures and can influence the successful establishment of patient tumor - 101 
derived cultures. We compared two commonly used tissue dissociating enzymes: Collagenase/Hyaluronidase 102 
(C&H), and dispase for the final yield, viability, abundance of tumor and immune cells, and e xpression of stress 103 
response genes ( Fig. S1, A). Adjacent tissue regions from each tumor were dissociated with C&H or dispase. 104 
Our results showed no significant difference in the final yield or calcein positive live cells between C&H and 105 
dispase -dissociation ( Fig. S1, B-D). Similarly, the abundance of CK7+ tumor cells or CD45+ immune cells 106 
were not affected by the nature of enzymes ( Fig. S1, E and F). However, the expressions of stress response 107 
genes ATF3 , FOS, and HSPA1 were increased in C&H dissociated cells compared to dispase ( Fig. S1, G). 108 
The upregulation of stress response genes has been linked to chemoresistance (24, 25 ) and therefore can 109 
distort drug response analys is from cancer cells. Hence, we continued with dispase -dissociation for 110 
establishing iPDCs ( Fig. 1, A). HGSC primary or omental metastatic tumor -dissociated cells were cultured in 111 
basement membrane extract (BME) or OmGel for up to one week. Compared to cells cultured in BME, OmGel 112

PARPi maintenance therapy (27). We tested if the PDCs established from treatment -naive patient -derived 122

subjected to the genomic ovaHRD scar test to evaluate homologs recombination ( HR) status (Table . S, 3) (28). 124

 DISCUSSION 283 
Here we established a novel patient -derived immunocompetent culture platform of HGSC tumors, and validate 284 
it using histologigy, genomic and single -cell analysis. Importantly, our results highlight that iPDCs mimic the 285 
clinical responses to PARPi , and chemotherapy treatments thus validating the utility of iPDCs for testing 286 
chemotherapy and targeted therapies. In line with the clinical responses to PARPi, we found increased tumor 287 
cell sensitivity of HRD -iPDCs to PARPi olaparib compared to HRP -iPDCs . Importantly, the in vitro iPDC 288 
response to PARPi significantly correlated with matched patient’s clinical responses particularly, PFS and the 289 
duration of PARPi treatment in the clinics. These results validate the utility of iPDCs for testing therapeutic 290 
agents from primary and therapy -resistant tumors. 291 
Tissue -specific matrices have been shown to improve the prediction of treatment responses by preserving the 292

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf::chunk_0020
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
inics. These results validate the utility of iPDCs for testing therapeutic 290 
agents from primary and therapy -resistant tumors. 291 
Tissue -specific matrices have been shown to improve the prediction of treatment responses by preserving the 292 
native ECM cues in pancreatic cancer and lymphoid metastases (21, 22 ). Previous studies on HGSC organoid 293 
cultures have mostly utilized mouse sarcoma derived BME matrix (37). Our result demonstrate that the unique 294 
OmGel provides an optimal growth environment recapitulating th e HGSC metastatic tumors. The proteomic 295 
analysis of OmGel by Neilson et al., report that there are no detectable levels of cytokines and growth factors 296 
in OmGel, suggesting that OmGel on its own does not alter the immune compartment of the iPDCs (23). In 297 
line with this we show that neither dispase digestion nor OmGel matrix alter the immune cells indicating that 298 
all the immune cells from the source tumors are faithfully recapitulated in the iPDCs, thereby making them an 299 
ideal model for the investigation of immunotherapy agents. As omentum is the most frequent site of 300 
intrabdominal metastases (38) our model with OmGel will be highly relevant for a broad range of 301 
gastrointestinal and gynecological cancers frequently metastasizing to the omentum. 302 
Thus far, high -throughput drug response profiling in HGSC has been performed predominantly on cancer cell 303 
cultures or organoi ds using compounds targeting oncogenic drivers. Herein we have established a patient - 304 
derived pre -clinical model and analysis pipelines that facilitate the investigation of immuno -oncology 305 
combinations in a high -throughput manner enabling the analysis of tr eatment responses to over 50 different 306
