derived pre -clinical model and analysis pipelines that facilitate the investigation of immuno -oncology 305 
combinations in a high -throughput manner enabling the analysis of tr eatment responses to over 50 different 306 
conditions on tumors from multiple patients. Moreover, while previous studies have mainly involved 307 
measurement of metabolic markers independent of the cell types, our single -cell image -based readout 308 
uniquely captures tumor and immune cell specific functional states. Although the evidence from clinical trials 309 
indicates unfavorable responses to ICIs in HGSC, these studies were conducted on unstratified patient 310 
populations (39). Here we provide novel insights that iPDCs established from treatment -naive tumors show 311 

## Page 14
 
 
14 
 tumor cell responses to pembrolizumab predominantly in HRD -iPDCs. However, none of our patients received 312 
ICIs and thus the identification of HGSC patients potentially responding to ICIs still needs further investigation. 313 
Although combination of ATRi with other DNA damaging agents such as PARPi, or WEE1i have been clinically 314 
tested in ovarian cancer (40, 41 ), the combination of ATRi with immunotherapy has not been investigated 315 
before. For the first time, we report that combination of ATRi with both investigational (ATXi, and PERKi), and 316 
established (anti -PD1 antibody) immunotherapies show patient -specific effects on tumor and immune cell 317 
viability. Furthermore, we found that higher concentrations (1µM or above) of ATRi are likely toxic as they 318 
caused both cancer and immune cell death in a subset of iPDCs. However, our results using lower 319

vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
fects on tumor and immune cell 317 
viability. Furthermore, we found that higher concentrations (1µM or above) of ATRi are likely toxic as they 318 
caused both cancer and immune cell death in a subset of iPDCs. However, our results using lower 319 
concentrations of ATRi (0.1µM) in combination w ith ATXi showed cancer cell -specific death without affecting 320 
the immune cell viability in a tumor -dependent manner. Importantly, tumor cell -death responses to this 321 
combination aligned with CD8+ T cell and dendritic cell activation. Together our results hig hlight ATRi plus 322 
immunotherapies as a promising new combination treatment and suggest that iPDCs are suitable for screening 323 
immuno -oncology compounds as well as for deeper analysis of functional immune -cell state dynamics. 324 
Our iPDC platform also enables identification of potential biomarkers for response and resistance to 325 
treatments. Combining the drug response from treatment resistant iPDCs with pathway analysis from scRNA - 326 
seq and mutation analysis from WES suggested possible mechanism of resistance to P ARPi. These include 327 
enrichment of cell cycle, G2M checkpoint, WNT, and TGFb pathways in PARPi resistant tumor, an acquired 328 
BRCA1 mutation likely leading to restoration of HR function. These findings support the concept of integrating 329 
drug sensitivity and r esistance profiling of iPDCs with molecular features from the corresponding tumors to 330 
reveal mechanism behind treatment responses to enable implementation of more effective treatment 331 
strategies. Furthermore, the results from t -CycIF corroborate previous st udies showing replication stress as a 332

reveal mechanism behind treatment responses to enable implementation of more effective treatment 331 
strategies. Furthermore, the results from t -CycIF corroborate previous st udies showing replication stress as a 332 
biomarker of sensitivity to ATRi and suggest that this approach aid in the investigation tissue -based biomarkers 333 
for potential combination therapies identified on iPDCs. Our data from iPDCs highlight that integration o f high - 334 
content single cell image analysis with immune cell functional states from flow cytometry enable assessment 335 
of tumor cell -specific cytotoxic and immune cell -specific functional responses for stratification into responders 336 
and non -responders. Togethe r, our novel HGSC patient -derived immunocompetent cultures in physiologically 337 
relevant matrix combined with patient -specific tumor infiltrating immune cells facilitates high -throughput testing 338 
of novel immuno -oncology agents and personalized combination th erapies in HGSC. 339 

## Page 15
 
 
15 
 The limitations of iPDCs include that they are short -term cultures lasting up to one week without the ability to 340 
passage over time. This is inherent to any relevant immuno -oncology ex vivo models that can sustain the 341 
viability and function al states of the diverse immune cell repertoire. Secondly, this platform only allows 342 
investigation of immunotherapy effects on intra -tumoral immune cells but not on the mobility and priming of 343 
the peripheral blood immune cells. Thirdly, the number of PARPi and resistant patients in our study is limited, 344 
due to the fact that these samples are difficult to get, and thus further validations using a larger number of 345 
recurrent samples are warranted.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf::chunk_0024

vector_chunks.text:
blood immune cells. Thirdly, the number of PARPi and resistant patients in our study is limited, 344 
due to the fact that these samples are difficult to get, and thus further validations using a larger number of 345 
recurrent samples are warranted. Despite these limitations, the fact that iPDCs harbor patient -matched 346 
intratumor immune cells makes them a useful preclinical model to evaluate effectiveness of immuno -oncology 347 
agents in activating intra -tumoral immune cells. Furthermore, our work highlight ATRi plus immunotherapy 348 
combinations as pote ntially effective agents in HGSC and demonstrate that coupling of iPDC drug responses 349 
with molecular and tissue -based features enable the discovery of predictive biomarkers. 350

 MATERIALS AND METHODS 351 
Patient material and clinical data 352 
All experiments involvi ng patient materials were performed in accordance with the ethical standards from the 353 
1975 Declaration of Helsinki. Tissue samples were collected as a part of ONCOSYS -OVA study (clinical trial 354 
number : NCT061173 84) from HGSC patients undergoing primary debu lking surgery (PDS) (n=41) or 355 
secondary surgery (n=3) at Helsinki University Hospital (HUH). Each patient gave written informed consent 356 
and the use of fresh tissue, blood, and formalin -fixed paraffin embedded (FFPE) HGSC clinical material was 357 
approved by the Ethics Committee of Women’s Clinic, HUH and Helsin ki Biobank. 358 
Tumor tissue processing 359 
In this study, w e processed HGSC tumors from 39 patients, depending on the availability tumor tissues were 360 
subjected to different experimental procedures listed in Table. S , 1. Freshly resected tumor tissue was cut into 361 
multiple pieces (approximately 100 mm3 per piece) .

The data from CellProfiler was graphically represented as the abundance of live tumor (D CM-, CK7+) and live 466 
immune cells (DCM -, CD45+) per treatment and per patient sample using RStudio. Outlier images which 467 
contained fewer than five cells, no CK7+ cells, and no CD45+ cells were filtered out. Images which contained 468 
an excessive number of cells, greater than three standard deviations above the mean, were also excluded. The 469 
data was visualized using a ratio of CK7 alive over all alive cells (tumor cell abundance), CD45 positive alive 470 
cells over all cells (immune cell abundance). The median tumor and immune abundance proportions per 471 
treatment were normalized by the control and the log2 fold change was plotted as the heatmap. 472

Dispase -dissociated single cell suspensions were washed in 1X PBS + 0.04% BSA solution and cell viability 474 
was assessed on an automated cell counter. scRNA -seq libraries were prepared with Chromium NextGEM 475 
Single Cell 3' reagent kit (10x Genomics) and sequenced on a NovaSeq 6000 ins trument (Sequencing Unit of 476 
Institute of Molecular Medicine Finland, Finland). scRNA -seq data preprocessing was done as described 477 
previously (25). Briefly, the FASTQ files were processed with the Cell Ranger pipeline v.6.0.1 (10x Genomics) 478 
to perform sample demultiplexing, alignment, barcode processing, and UMI quantification. The reference index 479

to perform sample demultiplexing, alignment, barcode processing, and UMI quantification. The reference index 479 
was built upon the GRCh38.d1.vd1 reference genome with GENCODE v25 annotation. The count matrices of 480 
four samples were loaded into Seurat R package (v.4.3.0) and merged, and the miQC algorithm (42) was used 481

immune cells) based on the expression of marker genes as described previously (25). To assign high - 483 
resolution immune cell types, the celltypist algorithm was used with the model "Immune_All_Low". 484 
The decoupleR R package (v.2.7.1) (43) was used to infer biologi cal activities of 14 signaling pathways 485 
available in PROGENy (44). For each sample, pathway activities across cell types were inferred by running 486 
the run_ulm function with default parameters, and aggregated pathway activities wer e visualized as a heatmap. 487

Statistical analysis 500 
Paired Wilcoxon signed -rank test was applied for comparison of organoid numbers between BME and 501 
OmeGel. Pearson correlation was employed for correlation analysis of the WES data. A p -value threshold of 502 
0.05 was set to determine significant statistical comparisons. Mann -Whitney U test was applied for quantified 503 
data from PDC tumor cell response to olaparib treatment in HRD vs HRP PDCs. Pearson’s correlation 504 
coefficient was applied to calculate the significance between patient’s PFS and PDC response to olaparib and 505 
Wilcoxon signed -rank test was applied for calculating the significance between duration of PARPi treatments 506 
in patient vs PDC response to olaparib. Paired Wilcoxon signed -rank test was used for comparison of immune 507 
cell proportions between C&H vs dispase , BME vs OmGel, and tumor vs iPDCs.

## Page 38
SUPPLEMENTARY MATERIALS AND METHODS 1 
WES data processing and analysis 2 
Bioinformatic processing of DNA samples involved several steps, including quality control, alignment to 3 
the reference human genome, deduplication, cross -sample contamination estimation, and variant 4 
discovery. Sequenced DNA reads underwent quality control a nd trimming steps using FastQC (47) and 5 
Trimmomatic tools (48). Subsequently, the high -quality reads were aligned to the reference human 6 
genome GRCh38.d1.vd1 using BWA -MEM (49) with default parameters, subjected to deduplication with 7

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf::chunk_0057
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/Nagaraj et al 2024 preprint.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
ol a nd trimming steps using FastQC (47) and 5 
Trimmomatic tools (48). Subsequently, the high -quality reads were aligned to the reference human 6 
genome GRCh38.d1.vd1 using BWA -MEM (49) with default parameters, subjected to deduplication with 7 
Picard tool and base quality recalibration using the Genome Analysis Toolkit (GATK) version 4.1.9.0 8 
(50). Cross -sample contamination estimation was conducted using GATK 4.1.9.0, with a contamination 9 
estimation threshold established at 15%. 10 
 11 
Mutation calling 12 
Somatic short variants were called through the collective analysis of multiple tumor samples against a 13 
singular matched normal for each patient, using GATK 4.1.4.1 Mutect2 according to established best 14 
practices. We used a Panel of Normal (PoN) generated with 181 normal samples from the prospective, 15 
longitudinal, multiregion observational study DECIDER (Multi -Layer Data to Improve Diagnosis, Predict 16 
Therapy Resistance and Suggest Targeted Therapies in HGSOC; ClinicalTrials.gov identifier 17 
NCT04846933) and 99 TCGA normals. A detailed description of PoN creation can be found in Lahtinen 18 
et al (51). Subsequently, GATK FilterMutectCalls was used for variant filtration, keeping only those 19 
variants that successfully passing all filters. Variant allele frequencies (VAF) were computed based on 20 
the read depths for reference and alternat e alleles (AD field). We annotated the using ANNOVAR 21 
20191024 (52). 22 
 23 
iPDC -tissue concordance evaluation 24 
To assess the alignment between iPDC and tissue samples, we calculated tissue -based concordance 25 
(Ct) by determining the ratio of shared mutations in both iPDC (i) and tissue samples (t) to mutations 26 
found in the tissue (t) Ct = (Mi ∩ Mt) / Mt.

vector_chunks.text:
assess the alignment between iPDC and tissue samples, we calculated tissue -based concordance 25 
(Ct) by determining the ratio of shared mutations in both iPDC (i) and tissue samples (t) to mutations 26 
found in the tissue (t) Ct = (Mi ∩ Mt) / Mt. Concordance analysis was conducted for iPDC -tissue pairs, 27 
with each pair consisting of iPDC and tissue samples derived from the same patient and organ. In total, 28 
10 iPDC -tissue pairs were analyzed. 29 
 30 

## Page 39
iPDC -tissue VAF correlation 31 
Like iPDC -tissue concordance, we computed organoid -tissue Variant Allele Frequency (VAF) 32 
correlation for 10 pairs composed of iPDC and tissue samples from the same patient. 33 
 34 
Mutation and pathway analysis of diagnostic and resistant samples 35 
Exonic mutations were co llected from both primary and relapse samples for patients C865 and C429 . 36 
The mutated genes identified from these samples were then subjected to over -representation analysis 37 
using the ConsensusPathDB algorithm (5 3). 38

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/adr2171_Blinded Review Report_seq1_v1.pdf::chunk_0000
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/adr2171_Blinded Review Report_seq1_v1.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
## Page 1
Review round: 2
Reviewer: 2
The understanding of the tumor microenvironment has led to several findings
on how we can reprogram the anti-tumor immunity and therapy. However,
the limitations on exploring this in human samples restriction how we can
identify suitable therapies. In this study, the author developed a new platform
to preserve the human tumor microenvironment and test if this be applied
for examining drug sensitivity. Despite the findings are well presented, this
study did not provide sufficient intellectual advances in this topic and I do not
support it to be published in Science Advances.
1. The lack of cell quantity and quality analysis in other sensitive format, such
as FACS, make this difficult to be appreciated. This is important for more
sensitive drug screening or examination.
2. There are other similar platforms have been developed. It is not clear how
this platform can be better or address unresolved issues other platforms can
do achieve based on the current data.
Reviewer: 3
The study by Nagaraj et al titled” Patient-derived functional immuno-oncology
platform identifies responders to ATR inhibitor and 1 immunotherapy
in ovarian cancer “, presents experimental data on new patient-derived
immunocompetent culture from ovarian cancer tissue (iPDC). After molecular
characterization of the novel model on the cellular and genomic level, authors
performed in vitro testing with PARP inhibitor Olaparib, followed by the
extensive testing of 3 selected patient lines with more drugs and correlation of
observed in vitro responses with available clinical data. Finally, the study goes
on to apply the combinatorial treatment on the set of lines in high throughput
384 well formats of diverse combinatorial treatments of immune-oncology
agents.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/adr2171_Blinded Review Report_seq1_v1.pdf::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/adr2171_Blinded Review Report_seq1_v1.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
lation of
observed in vitro responses with available clinical data. Finally, the study goes
on to apply the combinatorial treatment on the set of lines in high throughput
384 well formats of diverse combinatorial treatments of immune-oncology
agents. (ATRi, ATXi, anti PD1 and others) and claims to identify pRPA32 as a
putative biomarker of ATRi+ATX-sensitive tumors.
While in principle interesting as a new experimental platform, which includes
heterotypic cells, and is amenable to high throughput format, the presented
data does not provide sufficiently robust evidence of “proof of concept” to
support main conclusions and claims. Key readout used as a measure of drug
effectiveness, and representing the change in the number of tumor cells (ck7+),
quantified with single-cell imaging is not adequately described. Importantly,
the presented data is not fully convincing. While authors obtained borderline
significance in a statistical test of their choice, for analysis of the difference
in response to Olaparib between homologous deficient (HRP) and proficient
donor lines, the plot in 1E clearly shows that the distribution of values greatly
overlaps between the groups. Different types of statistical testing and likely
a larger number of samples would be necessary to identify cut-off values
and establish test specificity and sensitivity. As it is now, data shows, at the
very best a trend or tendency but they do not validate a test. It Is very likely

## Page 2
that without previous knowledge of the HRD status, it would be impossible
to predict the Olaparib response of the test sample only based on the in
vitro data. The manuscript could also greatly benefit from a more informative
description and concrete source data on how individual plots are generated.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/adr2171_Blinded Review Report_seq1_v1.pdf::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/adr2171_Blinded Review Report_seq1_v1.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
it would be impossible
to predict the Olaparib response of the test sample only based on the in
vitro data. The manuscript could also greatly benefit from a more informative
description and concrete source data on how individual plots are generated.
The argumentation for the selection of statistical tests should be explained.
For example, in Figure 1 J and K it is not clear which samples are included in
the analysis as supplemental data says that 18 patients in the cohort received
PARP inhibitor therapy. Also, the detailed drug responses of the 3 selected
patients in Figure 3 are not fully informative as the rationale for the selection
of the drugs is not entirely convincing. It would have been very interesting
to see what cisplatin response is also of C218 or C865 patients to see how
results from C429 directly compare even if the patients did not receive therapy
in the clinic. The extended transcriptional analysis of 3 patient samples, is
descriptive and in the absence of the possibility of testing key findings due to
limitations of short-term cultivation, remains inconclusive.
The last section of combinatorial testing is solely based on the interpretation
of the in vitro data so it is difficult to assess what is true translational potential.
Accordingly, the establishment of pRPA32 as a biomarker of ATRi response
can only be understood as hypothetical.
Overall, the study provides some valuable insights but the data is not robust
enough to constitute significant advancement in the field. Substantial revisions
would be necessary with new patient samples and likely larger cohorts of
tissue samples to properly asses the validity and robustness of the model.

The manuscript presents a novel method aimed at recapitulating the immune

Review round: 1
Reviewer: 1
The manuscript presents a novel method aimed at recapitulating the immune
microenvironment and maintaining tumor-derived immune cells through the
generation of iPDCs. While the method claims to effectively model these
immune features, several significant concerns have been identified that cast
doubt on the validity of these claims.
One of the primary issues is the consistency of immune features after
culturing, as discrepancies in the data suggest that these features may not be
preserved. For example, in Figure 2, the reported abundance of dendritic cells
is surprisingly high across most patients, which raises questions about the
accuracy of the results. The authors should provide the gating hierarchy and
standard biaxial plots to validate these findings. Additionally, a discrepancy
between single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) results and multi-color flow
cytometry data further undermines confidence in the immune profiling. The
scRNA-seq datasets show very few annotated myeloid dendritic cells (mDCs),
and there is a dramatic difference in the proportions of various immune
subsets between the two methods. This inconsistency is also evident in Figure
S2, where, in the case of the same patient (C342), dendritic cells constitute a
minimal proportion of immune cells in the dissociated tumor (as indicated by a

## Page 3
dark green bar) but make up about 50% of all immune cells after culturing (as
shown by a light green bar).
Moreover, there is a notable lack of detail regarding the immune-related
experiments. It is unclear, for instance, whether the interferon staining in

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/adr2171_Blinded Review Report_seq1_v1.pdf::chunk_0004
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/adr2171_Blinded Review Report_seq1_v1.pdf
vector_chunks.text:
een bar) but make up about 50% of all immune cells after culturing (as
shown by a light green bar).
Moreover, there is a notable lack of detail regarding the immune-related
experiments. It is unclear, for instance, whether the interferon staining in
Figures 2 and 4 was performed using a Golgi block, which would enhance
the robustness of the staining. The absence of biaxial plots or histograms
is problematic, especially since the observed fold changes are very small
(approximately log2FC = 0.2). A slight modification in gating could potentially
result in fold changes of a similar magnitude. Furthermore, the minimal
spanning tree in Figure S2d does not maintain consistency across different
markers, with the plot for CD14 appearing to come from a separate analysis
run.
Another critical concern is the small sample size used in the study. The entire
argument in Figure 3 is based on data from one stable patient and two resistant
patients, which is insufficient for drawing robust conclusions. Independent
validation with a larger sample size is necessary to support the findings.
Additionally, the methods for quantifying the data require clearer description
and justification to allow for a better evaluation of the data interpretation.
For example, in Figure 1H, the representative image shows higher dead cell
staining in a sensitive patient compared to a resistant patient even under
control conditions, suggesting that there might be intrinsic growth differences
independent of drug treatment. Furthermore, the treatment duration varies
between 4 to 10 days depending on cell growth, but the criteria for determining
the time points for data collection are not well defined. It is also unclear
whether the quantification varies significantly at different time points, which

ation varies
between 4 to 10 days depending on cell growth, but the criteria for determining
the time points for data collection are not well defined. It is also unclear

could impact the interpretation of the results.

Resubmission planning - comments and thoughts for going forward
Due to the rejection and Science Advances, we need to prepare the manuscript for resubmission in another journal. As the reviewer comments largely focus on the sample numbers and the robustness of the findings, we have to critically assess the entire manuscript to be able to turn it into a publishable story. 
Before we meet during week 46, we all (Anniina, Joonas, Maria, Matilda) should go through the manuscript critically, and assess its strengths and weaknesses in order to make it better.
Some questions to lead into the discussion on what we should change in the manuscript in order to prepare it for resubmission:
What are the advantages and strengths of our model vs. other similar models?
Why do we not want to go deeper into FACS etc. validation for our model?
Which journal should we aim for next?
New title: Platform for multimodal functional testing and biomarker discovery? 
Joonas
1. Our model is very clean in that we do not introduce additional cell components to the cultures in successive steps, we utilize only the material directly obtained from the patient. Also, cultrex has a quite small batch variation in its composition. However, is cultrex the most relevant for our purposes?
2. The current validations are problematic due to the high disparity between validation methods. I dont think introducing new data types will improve anything. I would more focus on why the current discrepancies exist and can we do something about them using our existing data
3. Clinical Cancer Res? 
JCI Insight? 
EMBO Mol Med?
4. Depends on what we display of the drug screening results (all, or do we cut back on some aspect?), and do we trust those results?
Matilda

can we do something about them using our existing data

EMBO Mol Med?
4. Depends on what we display of the drug screening results (all, or do we cut back on some aspect?), and do we trust those results?
Matilda
- Omentum gel matrix provides a more disease-specific growth environment for the cancer cells vs BME
- The preservation of immune cells from the patient samples (BUT how much do we trust the results from the immune cell scorings?)
We already have a bunch of validation methods in use, can we strengthen the proof from these instead of performing more validations? I think introducing more validations will only make the story more confusing. 
Cell Reports 2020:
Patient-Derived Ovarian Cancer Organoids Mimic Clinical Response and Exhibit Heterogeneous Inter- and Intrapatient Drug Responses
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32553164/
WGS data, no imaging results
I like the new title suggestion, I don’t think the ATRi results should be the focus of the paper. For me, the results I feel least confident in are the imaging data results in Fig 4
The amount of data we have is massive, and it was very difficult to normalise the data
Reviewer 3: “The absence of biaxial plots or histograms is problematic, especially since the observed fold changes are very small (approximately log2FC = 0.2). A slight modification in gating could potentially result in fold changes of a similar magnitude.”
None of the key people involved in this analysis work in the lab anymore (Ashwini, Aditi)
Maria

vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
Ex Vivo Immuno-Oncology Platform Reveals Spatial T Cell Infiltration Patterns Linked to ATR Inhibition Responses in High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer
Ashwini S Nagaraj1, Matilda Salko1†, Aditi Sirsikar1†, Ziqi Kang1, Erdogan Pekcan Erkan1,2, Elina A Pietilä1, Iga Niemiec1, Jie Bao1, Giovanni Marchi1, Angéla Szabó1, María Hincapié-Otero1, Anastasia Lundgren1, Kirsten Nowlan3, Sanna Pikkusaari1, Anna Kanerva4, Johanna Tapper4, Riitta Koivisto-Korander4, Heini Lassus1,4, Liisa Kauppi1, Sampsa Hautaniemi1, Anna Vähärautio1,5, Jing Tang1, Eliisa Kekäläinen3,6, Ulla-Maija Haltia1,4, Anni Virtanen1,7, Joonas Jukonen1, Tuula Salo8,9,10,11, Anniina Färkkilä1,12,13,14*
1 Research Program in Systems Oncology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland.
2 Current address: Faculty of Medicine and Health Technology, Tampere University and TAYS Cancer Centre, Tampere, Finland.
3 Department of Bacteriology and Immunology, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland.
4. Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland.
5 Foundation for the Finnish Cancer Institute, Finland.
6 Translational Immunology Research Program, Faculty of Medicine, University of Helsinki and Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland.
7 Department of Pathology, University of Helsinki and HUS Diagnostic Center, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland.
8 Department of Oral and Maxillofacial Diseases, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland.
9 Department of Pathology, HUSLAB, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland.
10 Unit of Population Health, Medical Research Center Oulu, Finland.
11 University of Oulu and Oulu University Hospital, Oulu, Finland.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0001
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland.
9 Department of Pathology, HUSLAB, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland.
10 Unit of Population Health, Medical Research Center Oulu, Finland.
11 University of Oulu and Oulu University Hospital, Oulu, Finland.
12 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, and Clinical trials unit, Comprehensive Cancer Centre, Helsinki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland.
13 iCAN digital precision cancer medicine, Helsinki, Finland.
14 Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Finland
 †Equal contribution
 *Correspondence: E-mail: anniina.farkkila@helsinki.fi, 
ABSTRACT
Identifying new therapeutic approaches in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC) requires the development of more accurate preclinical models that replicate the patient-specific tumor and its microenvironment. To address this, we established immunocompetent patient-derived cultures (iPDCs) for HGSC, cultured on a novel, physiologically relevant human omentum-gel matrix. We developed a high-throughput platform that combines drug testing, histological analysis, genomic profiling, single-cell studies, and spatial biomarker discovery. Our results from 47 tumors show that iPDCs faithfully recapitulate the tumor genomic and histological characteristics, also retaining the intratumoral immune cells. Importantly, the iPDC treatment responses correlate significantly with the patients’ clinical responses. Using scRNA-seq, we identify new therapeutic options linked to distinct mechanisms of resistance and tumor cell states in recurrent HGSC patients.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
mportantly, the iPDC treatment responses correlate significantly with the patients’ clinical responses. Using scRNA-seq, we identify new therapeutic options linked to distinct mechanisms of resistance and tumor cell states in recurrent HGSC patients. High-throughput drug testing with single cell-imaging identified ataxia telangiectasia and Rad3-related inhibitor (ATRi) combined with a novel immunotherapy targeting Autotaxin as a promising new combination treatment for HGSC. Using hyperplexed imaging and the SPACEstat analysis package, we discovered that ATRi responses were associated with significant increases in both intra- and peritumoral T cell infiltration, particularly in PD1+ CD8 T cells. Additionally, we observed enhanced –GrzB expression in PD1+ CD8 T cells, linked to spatial interactions with replication stress marker pRPA32+ tumor cells, which was further augmented upon ATRi treatment. In summary, we present proof of concept for our iPDC platform as a high-throughput, representative functional model to advance precision oncology in HGSC and propose ATRi-immunotherapy combination to be a potential target for future clinical studies.
INTRODUCTION
High-grade serous ovarian carcinoma is the most common subtype of ovarian cancer, often diagnosed at an advanced stage with 10-year survival of 15% (1). Nearly 80% of HGSCs metastasize to omentum, a large adipose connective tissue covering stomach and intestine, which provides a metabolically compatible and inflammatory microenvironment for HGSC (2). The standard of care for HGSC includes primary debulking surgery followed by platinum-based chemotherapy. Despite the successful response to initial treatment, recurrent disease often develops within the first three years.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0003
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
roenvironment for HGSC (2). The standard of care for HGSC includes primary debulking surgery followed by platinum-based chemotherapy. Despite the successful response to initial treatment, recurrent disease often develops within the first three years. Poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase inhibitors (PARPi) have improved patient outcomes when used as a maintenance treatment after first-line chemotherapy, particularly in patients with homologous recombination deficiency (HRD), and/or sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy (3). However, 35-45% of the patients discontinue the treatment due to resistance to PARPi (4,5), suggesting that novel combination strategies are required to overcome PARPi resistance. 
Single agent immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) have shown limited efficacy in HGSC (6). The lack of success with ICIs in HGSC is likely due to a variety of immunosuppressive cells and mechanisms in the tumor microenvironment (TME) (7). Targeting these mechanisms is one of the strategies to improve the efficacy of ICIs. Towards this, mouse model studies display the importance of enhancing CD8 T cell response via activating interferon signaling in dendritic cells (DCs) or by depleting myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSC), (8,9).
To improve the therapeutic benefit of ICIs, their combination with chemotherapy, PARPi, or anti-angiogenic agents are under clinical investigation (10,11). Interestingly, results from the TOPACIO Phase I/II clinical trial showed that a HRD surrogate signature predicts sensitivity to combination of PARPi, niraparib and pembrolizumab (12).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0004
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
r anti-angiogenic agents are under clinical investigation (10,11). Interestingly, results from the TOPACIO Phase I/II clinical trial showed that a HRD surrogate signature predicts sensitivity to combination of PARPi, niraparib and pembrolizumab (12). In addition, BRCA1/2-defective HGSC has been shown to exhibit increased immunosurveillance with more neoantigens, tumor infiltrating lymphocytes, programmed cell death (PD-1) and its ligand (PD-L1) in comparison to HRP tumors (13–15). These findings highlight the potential for novel immunotherapy combinations for patients with HGSC. Additionally, DNA damage response inhibitors (DDRi) including cell cycle checkpoint inhibitors are being clinically explored (16). While preclinical studies implicate the benefit of combining of DDRi and ICIs (17), investigation of novel combinations of ATRi with other DNA damaging agents or immunotherapies is required to guide personalized immuno-oncology in ovarian cancer. However, the efficacy of these agents needs to be validated in immunocompetent models representing patient tumor-specific immune microenvironment prior to clinical investigation.
A variety of patient-derived ex vivo models have been developed to model tumor immune microenvironment, and to evaluate response to immunotherapies (18–20). These studies have utilized mouse or rat-derived matrigel or collagen-based matrices to culture the cells or tissue fragments, in which the rodent-derived matrix may hinder the faithful recapitulation of the functional HGSC TME. Human tissue-specific extracellular matrices (ECM) have been produced to model the primary and metastatic TME (21,22).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0005
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
he cells or tissue fragments, in which the rodent-derived matrix may hinder the faithful recapitulation of the functional HGSC TME. Human tissue-specific extracellular matrices (ECM) have been produced to model the primary and metastatic TME (21,22). Towards this, Neilson et al., have generated and characterized a human omentum gel matrix (OmGel) from tumor-free omentum, which has the potential utility for studying omental metastatic microenvironment across a plethora of tumor types with frequent metastasis to omentum (23). Here we established a functional precision immuno-oncology platform using novel HGSC patient-derived immunocompetent cultures (iPDCs) using patient-derived OmGel matrix. We validated that the iPDCs preserve tumor and immune cell features of the native tumor and recapitulate the clinical responses to chemotherapy and PARPis. Importantly, by coupling single cell image-based drug responses with immune cell functional states we show that iPDCs reveal patient-specific vulnerabilities to ATRi combined with novel immunotherapies. Interestingly, the responses to ATRi associate with enhanced intratumoral PD1+ CD8 T cell infiltration and activation linked to spatially interacting, replication stress-positive tumor cell phenotypes in HGSC. 
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Patient material and clinical data
All experiments involving patient materials were performed in accordance with the ethical standards from the 1975 Declaration of Helsinki. Tissue samples were collected as a part of ONCOSYS-OVA study (clinical trial number: NCT06117384) from HGSC patients undergoing primary debulking surgery (PDS) (n=41) or secondary surgery (n=3) at Helsinki University Hospital (HUH).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0006
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
tion of Helsinki. Tissue samples were collected as a part of ONCOSYS-OVA study (clinical trial number: NCT06117384) from HGSC patients undergoing primary debulking surgery (PDS) (n=41) or secondary surgery (n=3) at Helsinki University Hospital (HUH). Each patient gave written informed consent, and the use of fresh tissue, blood, and formalin-fixed paraffin embedded (FFPE) HGSC clinical material was approved by the Ethics Committee of Women’s Clinic, HUH and Helsinki Biobank.
Tumor tissue processing 
Altogether, we processed 47 HGSC tumors from 44 patients and established iPDCs from 37 tumors (STable 1). Freshly resected tumor tissue was cut into multiple pieces (approximately 100 mm3 per piece). Three to six pieces were snap frozen in liquid nitrogen for DNA, and RNA, isolation, one piece was fixed in 10% formalin for histopathology, and t-CycIF analysis. Depending on the availability, 100 mm3-2000 mm3 cm tumor tissue piece was minced into smaller pieces and digested with 1x Collagenase & Hyaluronidase (# 7912, Stem Cell Technologies), or 1U/mL Dispase II (# D4693, Sigma/Merck), in ADF+++ medium (DMEM F12, (# 11320-074 Thermo Scientific) supplemented with GlutaMAX™-I (100X) (#35050-061, GibcoLifeTechnologies), Penicillin-Streptomycin (10,000 U/ml), (#15140122 GibcoLifeTechnologies), and HEPES, (# H0887, Sigma)), and 50 U/ mL DNase (# M6101, Promega) at 37°C for 1 hr rotating incubator. Digested tissue was filtered through 70µM strainer and centrifuged at 180 g for 10 min at +4°C. The pellet was subjected to RBC lysis for 5 min at room temperature (RT) followed by dilution in AdF+++ medium and centrifuged at 180 g for 10 min at +4°C. Cells in the resulting final pellet was counted with trypan blue using LUNA-FL™ Dual Fluorescence Cell Counter.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0007
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ected to RBC lysis for 5 min at room temperature (RT) followed by dilution in AdF+++ medium and centrifuged at 180 g for 10 min at +4°C. Cells in the resulting final pellet was counted with trypan blue using LUNA-FL™ Dual Fluorescence Cell Counter. 
Viability and Immunofluorescence analysis on the dissociated cells
Enzymatically dissociated cells were plated on to Shandon™ Multi-Spot Slides (#9991090, ThermoFisher Scientific) at a concentration of 30000 cells / 3µL and allowed to air dry at RT. Cells were stained with LIVE/DEAD™ Viability/Cytotoxicity Kit, for mammalian cells (# L3224, Thermo Fisher Scientific), and incubated at 37oC for 30 min, or cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min at RT. Blocking was performed with 2% TritonX-100+3%BSA in 1xPBS for 30 min at room temperature cells were stained with Anti-Cytokeratin 7 antibody [EPR17078] (Alexa Fluor® 555, ab209601), and anti-CD45 [HI30] (Alexa Fluor® 647, #304018 Bio Legend) for 1hr at RT. Images were acquired using Nikon SlideExpress 2 microscope, followed by image analysis using Cell Profiler. The quantified data was plotted using GraphPad Prism.
Quantitative real-time PCR (RT-qPCR)
RNA was extracted from the cells dissociated with C&H or dispase or from fresh frozen tissues using RNeasy Plus Mini kit (#74134, Qiagen). 500 ng of RNA from each sample was used for cDNA synthesis using High-capacity RNA-to-cDNA kit (#4387406, Thermo Fisher Scientific). qPCR mixture was prepared using PowerUp™ SYBR™ Green Master Mix for qPCR (#A25741, Thermo Fisher Scientific), and ran using BioRad machine CFX 384. The quantified data was plotted using GraphPad Prism.
Establishment of patient-derived cultures
Patient-derived OmGel was prepared as previously described (23).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0008
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
r Mix for qPCR (#A25741, Thermo Fisher Scientific), and ran using BioRad machine CFX 384. The quantified data was plotted using GraphPad Prism.
Establishment of patient-derived cultures
Patient-derived OmGel was prepared as previously described (23). HGSC tumor-dissociated cells were mixed with Cultrex RGF Basement Membrane Extract (BME), Type 2 (# 3533-010-02 R&D Systems), or 2.5 mg/mL OmGel at a concentration of 1000 cells / µL and plated as a droplet in 48-well or 96 well plates. OmGel mixture was prepared as follows: appropriate volume of cell suspension was mixed with, 2.5 mg/mL OmGel, 33.3 ug/mL aprotinin (# A6279 Sigma), 0.3 U thrombin (#T6884 Sigma), and 0.5 µg/mL of fibrinogen (# 341578 Sigma). Addition of fibrinogen initiates solidification of the gel, hence it was added just before plating the suspension. Droplets containing BME or OmGel resuspended cells were allowed to solidify at 37 0C incubator for 1 hr. iPDC culture medium II (STable 1) supplemented with growth factors and cytokines was added on top of the droplets and cultured for 4-10 days depending on the sample.
Quantification of number of iPDCs per sample
On day 4-10 of the BME or OmGel cultures, bright-field images from 4 wells were taken at 10x magnification, and the number of cell clusters >3000 µm2 was counted using ImageJ-Fiji. The data was plotted using GraphPad Prism.
Whole-exome sequencing
iPDCs were harvested using Cultrex Organoid Harvesting solution; # 3700-100-01), and DNA was extracted using AllPrep DNA/RNA Mini Kit (Qiagen). 50ng of gDNA from blood (germline, n=11), fresh frozen tumor (n=13), FFPE tumor tissue (n=2), and iPDC (n=10) samples were subjected to WES. WES libraries were prepared using Twist Comprehensive Exome according to the manufacturer's instructions.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0009
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
n). 50ng of gDNA from blood (germline, n=11), fresh frozen tumor (n=13), FFPE tumor tissue (n=2), and iPDC (n=10) samples were subjected to WES. WES libraries were prepared using Twist Comprehensive Exome according to the manufacturer's instructions. This process involved the enrichment of exonic regions, capturing a comprehensive representation of the protein-coding regions of the human genome. Prepared libraries were subjected to high throughput sequencing with Illumina NovaSeq 6000 system using S4 flow cell (Illumina, San Diego, CA, USA) and v1.5 chemistry. DNA samples were subjected to quality control, alignment to the reference human genome, deduplication, estimation of cross-sample contamination, and discovery of genetic variants. We assessed organoid-tissue mutations concordance and Variant allele frequency (VAF) correlation using 10 organoid-tissue pairs. In addition, the pathway analysis from WES data of matched primary and relapse tumors were analyzed using the ConsensusPathDB algorithm. Details of the data processing and analyses can be found in the Supplementary Methods.
Flow cytometry and data analysis
Tumor tissue or iPDCs were dissociated using Dispase II (# D4693, Sigma/Merck), and the cells were stained with two different antibody panels.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0010
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
he data processing and analyses can be found in the Supplementary Methods.
Flow cytometry and data analysis
Tumor tissue or iPDCs were dissociated using Dispase II (# D4693, Sigma/Merck), and the cells were stained with two different antibody panels. First immune cell panel for comparison of dissociation methods consisted of the following antibodies: anti-CD14 PE (18D11, #21620144, ImmunoTools), anti-HLA-Dr PerCP-CY5.5 (G46-6, #552764 BD), anti-CD8 PE-Cy7 (RPA-T8, #557746, BD), anti-CD4 APC (MEM,241 #21270046, ImmunoTools), anti-CD56 AF700 (B159, #557919, BD), anti-Epcam APC/Fire 750 (9C4, #324233, Nordic BioSite), anti-CD45 BV421 (HI30, #563880, BD), anti-CD16 BV510 (3G8, #563829 BD), anti-CD19 BV605 (HIB19, #302243, BD), anti-CD11c, BV11c (B-ly6, #563130, FisherScientific), anti-CD3 BV786 (CD3 SK7, #563800, BD), dead cell marker (DCM) FITC (#L2301,Thermo Fisher Scientific). Second panel for the analysis of the functional states of the immune cells: anti-CD56 FITC (MEM-188, #21270563, ImmunoTools), anti-CD4 PE-CF594 (RPA-T4, #562281, BD), anti-Ki67 PE-CY7 (B56, #561283, BD), anti-Granzyme APC (GB11, #GRB05, Thermo Fisher Scientific), anti-IFN γ AF700 (B27, #557995, BD), DCM BV510 (#L34965 Thermo Fisher Scientific), and the remaining antibodies were the same as in the immune cell profiling panel. Samples were run using BD LSRFortessa™ Cell Analyzer. From the flow cytometry data, DCMneg and EpCAM neg cells were gated using FlowJo and exported as csv files for further analysis using CYTO (SFig 2A) (24). The proportions of the cell types from the clusters generated in CYTO were plotted using R-script.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0011
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
m the flow cytometry data, DCMneg and EpCAM neg cells were gated using FlowJo and exported as csv files for further analysis using CYTO (SFig 2A) (24). The proportions of the cell types from the clusters generated in CYTO were plotted using R-script. For the analysis of functional states of immune cells following treatment with olaparib or pembrolizumab, DCM- EpCAM- cells were imported into CYTO and clustered into 25 clusters using FlowSOM. Clusters were annotated into CD8+ T cells, CD4+ T cells, CD56+ NK cells, or CD11c+ myeloid cells based on the z-score of the cell type-specific markers per cluster. The annotated clusters were analyzed for log2FC in the mean intensity of GrzB, IFNγ, Ki67, and HLA-Dr per individual cell type in each treatment normalized to control.
The cell type annotation was performed using TRIBUS (25). Outliers were removed using the 99.9th percentile for input marker intensity, normalized with arcsin transformation with a cofactor of 5, and scaled by Z-score. Tribus was run with the following parameter settings of sigma=1, learning_rate=1, clustering_threshold=10, undefined_threshold=0.0005, and other_threshold=0.3. A total of 5 cell types were annotated, including NK cells (CD56+), Monocytes (CD14+), Myeloids (CD11c+), T cells (CD3+ CD8-), and T cells (CD3+ CD8+).
Drug treatment and immunofluorescence staining
Dissociated patient-derived cells were embedded as 1000 cells/µL in 25µL of OmGel matrix on a CellCarrier 384 Ultra microplates, PerkinElmer, or in 10µL of OmGel matrix on a Cell Culture 96 well F-bottom microplate, Greiner. Drops were left to solidify for 1 hr at +37°C before adding iPDC-growth medium. Next day, cultures were treated with olaparib (PARPi, AZD2281;

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0012
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text: icroplates, PerkinElmer, or in 10µL of OmGel matrix on a Cell Culture 96 well F-bottom microplate, Greiner. Drops were left to solidify for 1 hr at +37°C before adding iPDC-growth medium. Next day, cultures were treated with olaparib (PARPi, AZD2281; SelleckChem), pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1, Selleckchem), ziritaxestat (ATXi, GLPG1690; MedChemExpress), AMG PERK44 (PERKi; MedChemExpress), Adavosertib (WEE1i, MK-1775; SelleckChem) or berzosertib (ATRi, VE-822; SelleckChem) as single agents or as combinations using TECAN D300e Digital Dispenser (TECAN) in a randomized manner. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was used as a control. Chemo- and PARPi-resistant patient-derived iPDCs were cultured on a Shandon Multi-Spot slides (#9991090, ThermoScientific) and treated with cisplatin (S1166; SelleckChem) or Olaparib, WEE1i or ATRi. Drug treatment was carried out for 3-6 days, depending on the growth of iPDCs, as evaluated by phase-contrast microscopy. At the end of drug treatment, cells were stained with live-or-dead stain following manufacturer's protocol (#32004, Biotium) before fixing with 4% paraformaldehyde for 1hr at RT. Wells were washed 3x with 1x PBS followed by permeabilization and blocking with freshly prepared 3% bovine serum albumin and 0.3% Triton-100 in 1x PBS for 1hr at RT. After removing the blocking buffer, cells were incubated with antibody cocktail containing Anti-Cytokeratin 7 antibody [EPR17078] (Alexa Fluor® 555 conjugated, ab209601), anti-CD45 [HI30] (Alexa Fluor® 647 conjugated, #304018 Bio Legend) and Hoechst (#3342, Thermo Fisher Scientific) in the blocking buffer overnight at +40C. Next day wells were carefully washed 3 times with 1x PBS to remove all the TritonX-100 and unbound antibody mix. Wells were left with 50 µL of PBS for imaging.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0013
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
) and Hoechst (#3342, Thermo Fisher Scientific) in the blocking buffer overnight at +40C. Next day wells were carefully washed 3 times with 1x PBS to remove all the TritonX-100 and unbound antibody mix. Wells were left with 50 µL of PBS for imaging.
High-content single-cell imaging and data analysis
Images of the drug-treated samples (10-17 images per well) were acquired using Opera Phenix High Content Screening System (PerkinElmer) with 40x objective and Andor Zyla sCMOS camera with Harmony High Content Imaging and Analysis Software (PerkinElmer). Raw image processing was done with BIAS (Biology Image Analysis Software). Cell segmentation and quantification of tumor (CK7+), immune cells (CD45+), and dead cells (DCM+), was performed using CellProfiler. Sample-specific threshold was set for CK7 integrated intensity values from the membrane, CD45 mean intensity values from the membrane, and mean intensity values from the nucleus of DCM per sample for further analyses.
The data from CellProfiler was graphically represented as the abundance of live tumor (DCM-, CK7+) and live immune cells (DCM-, CD45+) per treatment and per patient sample using RStudio. Outlier images which contained fewer than five cells, no CK7+ cells, and no CD45+ cells were filtered out. Images which contained an excessive number of cells, greater than three standard deviations above the mean, were also excluded. The data was visualized using a ratio of CK7 alive over all alive cells (tumor cell abundance), CD45 positive alive cells over all cells (immune cell abundance). The median tumor and immune abundance proportions per treatment were normalized by the control and the log2 fold change was plotted as the heatmap. 
Single-cell RNA-sequencing and data analysis

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0014
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ive alive cells over all cells (immune cell abundance). The median tumor and immune abundance proportions per treatment were normalized by the control and the log2 fold change was plotted as the heatmap. 
Single-cell RNA-sequencing and data analysis
Dispase-dissociated single cell suspensions were washed in 1X PBS + 0.04% BSA solution and cell viability was assessed on an automated cell counter. scRNA-seq libraries were prepared with Chromium NextGEM Single Cell 3' reagent kit (10x Genomics) and sequenced on a NovaSeq 6000 instrument (Sequencing Unit of Institute of Molecular Medicine Finland, Finland). scRNA-seq data preprocessing was done as described previously (26). Briefly, the FASTQ files were processed with the Cell Ranger pipeline v.6.0.1 (10x Genomics) to perform sample demultiplexing, alignment, barcode processing, and UMI quantification. The reference index was built upon the GRCh38.d1.vd1 reference genome with GENCODE v25 annotation. The count matrices of four samples were loaded into Seurat R package (v.4.3.0) and merged, and the miQC algorithm (27) was used for quality control. Cells were assigned to the low-resolution cell types (epithelial tumor cells, stromal cells, and immune cells) based on the expression of marker genes as described previously (26). To assign high-resolution immune cell types, the celltypist algorithm was used with the model "Immune_All_Low". 
The decoupleR R package (v.2.7.1) (28) was used to infer biological activities of 14 signaling pathways available in PROGENy (29). For each sample, pathway activities across cell types were inferred by running the run_ulm function with default parameters, and aggregated pathway activities were visualized as a heatmap.
t-CycIF staining and image analysis

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0015
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ailable in PROGENy (29). For each sample, pathway activities across cell types were inferred by running the run_ulm function with default parameters, and aggregated pathway activities were visualized as a heatmap.
t-CycIF staining and image analysis
Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tumor tissue sections from 12 PDS patient samples were sequentially stained with validated antibodies (STable 3) and scanned using RareCyte Cyte Finder as outlined in the t-CycIF protocol (30). Images from each cycle of staining were stitched and registered to create one high-plex image using the ASHLAR algorithm (31). Nuclear segmentation was achieved with the StarDist method by identifying positive nuclear Hoechst staining (32). The mean fluorescence intensity for all markers expressed within each cell was quantified using an in-house python script. Quality control was carried out in CyLinter (https://labsyspharm.github.io/cylinter/) to remove data affected by staining and image processing artifacts, including folds in the tissue, antibody aggregates and shifted cells. To mitigate batch effects and enable data comparison, the raw intensity values from each sample was subjected to a log2 transformation followed by z-score normalization, the mean intensity of normalized pRPA32/RPA2(Ser8) in PAX8+ and or CK7+ tumor cells per sample were plotted. 
The spatial analysis of t-CycIF images was performed by an in-house package, SPACEstat (https://github.com/Kkkzq/SPACEstat). To define whether the immune cells resided in the tumor or stroma region, we calculated the proportion of cell types within a radius of 65µm (200 pixels). If > 50% of the detected cells were cancer cells, the immune cell was labeled as tumor-resident; otherwise, stroma-resident.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0016
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
cells resided in the tumor or stroma region, we calculated the proportion of cell types within a radius of 65µm (200 pixels). If > 50% of the detected cells were cancer cells, the immune cell was labeled as tumor-resident; otherwise, stroma-resident. The immune infiltration index was defined as immune density (tumor) divided by immune density (stroma), where the immune density in each region was calculated by the immune cell count in each region divided by the total number of tumor/stroma cells. The tumor stroma interface (TSI) was defined using KDTree-based nearest neighbor search. For each tumor cell, we detected the neighboring cells from the stroma located within a radius of 13 µm (40 pixels), and vice versa. For the immune-TSI distance analysis, we calculated the nearest distance from each immune cell to the TSI cells. The median distance of each immune cell type per slide was then calculated for statistical tests. Due to our limited sample size (n=6 per group), and based on prior biological expectation from previous experiments, we used the one-sided Mann–Whitney U test. When checking the spatial co-localization of functional markers, we went through each tumor cell and calculated the mean marker intensity within a radius of 65µm (200 pixels), after which we calculated the median value per slide. Statistical tests were performed accordingly. The selection of radius was based on the cell-cell distance distribution of the t-CycIF dataset. 
Statistical analysis
Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test was applied for comparison of organoid numbers between BME and OmeGel. Pearson correlation was employed for correlation analysis of the WES data. A p-value threshold of 0.05 was set to determine significant statistical comparisons.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0017
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
n signed-rank test was applied for comparison of organoid numbers between BME and OmeGel. Pearson correlation was employed for correlation analysis of the WES data. A p-value threshold of 0.05 was set to determine significant statistical comparisons. Mann-Whitney U test was applied for quantified data from PDC tumor cell response to olaparib treatment in HRD vs HRP PDCs. Pearson’s correlation coefficient was applied to calculate the significance between patient’s PFS and PDC response to olaparib and Wilcoxon signed-rank test was applied for calculating the significance between duration of PARPi treatments in patient vs PDC response to olaparib. Paired Wilcoxon signed-rank test was used for comparison of immune cell proportions between C&H vs dispase, BME vs OmGel, and tumor vs iPDCs. Wilcoxon signed-rank test was used to compare the proportions of CK7+ tumor or CD45+ immune cells in each treatment against the control.
Data availability statement
The scRNAseq data were produced by the authors, and the read count data was deposited to GEO with the accession number GSE307725. Other data were generated by the authors and available on request.
RESULTS
Humanized OmGel matrix significantly improves the growth of HGSC patient-derived cultures 
To enable faithful recapitulation of the TME in patient-derived cultures (PDCs), we first established an optimized tissue dissociation protocol via comparing two commonly used tissue dissociation enzymes for adjacent tissue regions: Collagenase/Hyaluronidase (C&H), and dispase for the final yield, viability, abundance of tumor and immune cells, and expression of stress response genes, to optimize tumor- and immune cell yield from the source tissue (SFig 1A).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx::chunk_0018
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Clean version of Revised Nagaraj et al Main Text.docx
vector_chunks.text: cent tissue regions: Collagenase/Hyaluronidase (C&H), and dispase for the final yield, viability, abundance of tumor and immune cells, and expression of stress response genes, to optimize tumor- and immune cell yield from the source tissue (SFig 1A). Our results showed no significant difference in the yield or viability between C&H and dispase dissociation (SFig 1B-D). Similarly, the abundance of CK7+ tumor cells or CD45+ immune cells were not affected by dissociation enzyme choice (SFig 1E,F). However, the expressions of stress response genes ATF3, FOS, and HSPA1 were significantly increased in C&H-dissociated cells compared to dispase-dissociated cells (SFig 1G). The upregulation of stress response genes has been linked to chemoresistance (26,33) and therefore can distort drug response analysis from cancer cells, and could lead to altered immune cell phenotypes. Hence, we continued with dispase dissociation for establishing the PDCs (Fig 1A). To assess the effect of the ECM on the growth of the PDCs, dissociated cells from HGSC primary or omental metastatic tumors were cultured in basement membrane extract (BME) or HGSC patient-derived OmGel for up to one week. Compared to cells cultured in BME, OmGel cultured cells showed significantly improved growth as measured by the number of PDCs with size >3000 µm2 (Fig 1B,C; SFig 1H). Importantly, OmGel cultured PDCs maintained source tumor morphology and expression of tumor-specific marker PAX8 (Fig 1D; SFig 1I). In addition, whole exome sequencing (WES) analysis of tumor and matched PDCs (n=10) showed a strong correlation (r=0.86) of the variant allele frequencies (VAF), and a high median concordance (95.8%) for tumor-specific mutations between tumor and PDC pairs (Fig 1E; SFig 1J; STable 2).
