vector_chunks.text:
of ATRi could yield tumor-cell specific cytotoxic effects. We selected ATXi for further validation and functional analysis in combination with ATRi as this agent is known to induce CD8 T cell activation via inducing IFN-signaling in cancer cells (9). In iPDCs established from 6 new HGSC patients, we observed that 3/5 iPDCs were sensitive to the lower dose combination of ATRi with ATXi without causing a significant reduction in live immune cells (Fig 4D,E). Importantly, this sensitivity was associated with an increased CD8 T cell activation indicated by higher GrzB and IFNγ, along with CD11c+ myeloid cell activation with increased IFNγ in 3/4 of the sensitive iPDCs (SFig 4B). These results confirm the efficacy and immune activation of the ATRi treatment in HGSC, highlighting the added value of our iPDC platform in identifying tumor cell-specific cytotoxic responses and also immune cell functional states during new single-agent and combination therapies. 
Tumor - CD8 T cell spatial and functional crosstalk underlies responses to ATRi 
To explore the spatial tumor microenvironment for biomarker discovery, we performed tissue cyclic multiplex immunofluorescence (t-CycIF) (30) on the corresponding formalin-fixed paraffin embedded (FFPE) tumor tissues using 15 markers cell types and functional states (Fig 4A; STable 3). Image analysis and single-cell quantification identified distinct tumor and immune cell populations including myeloid cells, CD8 T cells and CD8-/CD4 T cells. Interestingly, the majority of iPDCs responding to the ATRi combinations were characterized by higher levels of replication stress, indicated by increased pRPA32-RPA2(Ser8) expression in the tumor cells (Fig 4F,G).

vector_chunks.text:
, CD8 T cells and CD8-/CD4 T cells. Interestingly, the majority of iPDCs responding to the ATRi combinations were characterized by higher levels of replication stress, indicated by increased pRPA32-RPA2(Ser8) expression in the tumor cells (Fig 4F,G). This finding is in line with previous results from a Phase II clinical trial suggesting that replication stress is a predictor of ATRi response (38). To explore the spatial tumor-immune landscapes, we developed an integrative spatial package SPACEstat. SPACEstat utilizes the distribution of cell-cell nearest-neighbour distances, and calculates an immune infiltration index quantifying the relative enrichment of immune cells in intratumoral regions compared to stromal regions (SFig 4 C; methods). With SPACEstat we computationally defined the tumor-stroma interface (TSI) and quantified the distribution of cellular distances to the TSI. SPACEstat enables statistical assessment of immune infiltration patterns both globally and across the TSI (Fig 5A). 
Analysis of the iPDC source tumors revealed that tumors responsive to ATRi exhibited a significantly higher intratumoral immune infiltration, mostly driven by the intratumoral PD1+ CD8 T cells (Fig 5B; SFig 4C,D). Moreover, immune cells, and specifically the T cells and PD1+ CD8 T cells were located significantly closer to the stromal side of the TSI in the responders as compared to the non-responders (Fig 5B; SFig 4E-G). This suggests increased infiltration and aggregation of PD1+ CD8 T cells at the TSI in tumors responding to ATRi (Fig 5B; SFig 4E). Interestingly, the overall expression levels of GrzB and PD1 were significantly higher in the immune cells of tumors responding to ATRi (Fig 5C,D).

vector_chunks.text:
ation and aggregation of PD1+ CD8 T cells at the TSI in tumors responding to ATRi (Fig 5B; SFig 4E). Interestingly, the overall expression levels of GrzB and PD1 were significantly higher in the immune cells of tumors responding to ATRi (Fig 5C,D). 
To assess the spatial associations of tumor cell and intratumoral CD8 T cell states, we next assessed the pRPA32-stratified tumor neighbourhoods and PD1+ CD8 T cell GrzB expression levels (Fig 5E). Interestingly, we found that GrzB expression was significantly higher in PD1+ CD8 T cells proximity of pRPA32+ tumor cells as compared to pRPA32- tumor cells (Fig 5F; SFig 4H; SFig 5), suggesting enhanced local activation of CD8 T cells upon spatial proximity to pRPA32 tumor cells. 
In summary, our single-cell analysis revealed that ATRi-sensitive tumors were characterized by elevated tumor cell pRPA32 expression (Fig 5G). Further, SPACEstat-enabled statistical assessment revealed enhanced peri and intratumoral infiltration of PD1+ CD8 T cells. Importantly, spatial interactions of intratumoral PD1+ CD8 T cells with pRPA32+ tumor cells associated with enhanced GrzB activity potentially priming the spatial TME to respond to ATRi. Together, our spatial and functional results suggest a pre-existing intra and peritumoral CD8 T cell - spatial activation phenotype (Fig 5G), which is further augmented in IPDCs upon ATR inhibition in tumors responding to ATR. 
DISCUSSION
Here we established a proof-of-concept patient-derived immunocompetent culture platform of HGSC tumors and validated it using histology, genomic and single-cell as well as spatial analysis. Our results show that iPDCs faithfully recapitulate the source tumors, and mirror the clinical treatment responses.

vector_chunks.text:
immunocompetent culture platform of HGSC tumors and validated it using histology, genomic and single-cell as well as spatial analysis. Our results show that iPDCs faithfully recapitulate the source tumors, and mirror the clinical treatment responses. Using high-throughput single-cell response profiling, we identify responders to ATR inhibitors alone and in combination with novel immunotherapeutic agents. Moreover, using SPACEstat spatial analysis we uncover distinct tumor-immune functional crosstalk underpinning responses to ATRi, suggesting a role for T cell activation upon responses to ATRi, and highlighting the potential of our iPDC platform in advancing precision oncology in HGSC.
Tissue-specific matrices have been shown to improve the prediction of treatment responses by preserving the native ECM cues in pancreatic cancer and lymphatic metastases (21,22). Previous studies on HGSC organoid cultures have mostly utilized mouse sarcoma-derived BME matrix (45). Our results demonstrate that the unique OmGel provides an optimal growth environment recapitulating the TME of metastatic HGSC. Importantly, previous proteomic analysis confirmed that OmGel consists of key extracellular matrix components closely mimicking human omental tissue, with no detectable levels of cytokines or growth factors in OmGel (23). In line with this, we show that neither the digestion method nor OmGel matrix alters immune cell phenotypes, indicating that all the immune cells from the source tumors are faithfully recapitulated in the iPDCs. As the omentum is the most frequent site of intraperitoneal metastases also in other tumor types (46), our OmGel iPDC model presents as highly relevant for a broad range of gynecological and gastrointestinal cancers.

vector_chunks.text:
lly recapitulated in the iPDCs. As the omentum is the most frequent site of intraperitoneal metastases also in other tumor types (46), our OmGel iPDC model presents as highly relevant for a broad range of gynecological and gastrointestinal cancers. 
Thus far, high-throughput drug response profiling in HGSC has focused on monotypic cancer cell cultures or organoids, using compounds that target oncogenic drivers (47). Moreover, while previous studies have mainly involved measurement of metabolic markers such as ATP irrespective of the cell types in the cultures (48), our single-cell image-based readout uniquely captures tumor and immune cell specific functional states. Importantly, the iPDC responses recapitulate previous findings from clinical trials correlating HRD and PD-1 response (12), as well as replication stress and responses to ATRi (38), and in vivo responses in syngeneic mouse models of HGSC (9). Moreover, the iPDC responses were closely aligned with the patients clinical responses,highlighting the potential or our platform to identify new treatment options for HGSC. However, evaluating the clinical relevance of the therapy options presented in our study will require further prospective validation. 
ATR inhibitors have been clinically explored in combination with other DNA-damaging agents, such as PARPi and WEE1 inhibitors, in ovarian cancer (45,46). However, the combination of ATR inhibitors with immunotherapy has not been previously investigated.We herein report that combination of ATRi with both investigational (ATXi), and established (anti-PD1 antibody) immunotherapies showed responses in a subset of patients.

vector_chunks.text: nation of ATR inhibitors with immunotherapy has not been previously investigated.We herein report that combination of ATRi with both investigational (ATXi), and established (anti-PD1 antibody) immunotherapies showed responses in a subset of patients. Furthermore, we found that higher concentrations (1µM or above) of ATRi were toxic to both cancer and immune cells, and lowering concentrations ATRi (0.1µM) in combination with ATXi resulted in cancer cell-specific cytotoxicity without affecting immune cell viability. Importantly, tumor cell-death responses to this combination were aligned with CD8 T cell and CD11c+ myeloid cell activation. Importantly, our biomarker analysis using single-cell spatial profiling uncovered significantly different T cell infiltration patterns in ATRi responders. Moreover, we identified a link between tumor replication stress signaling and immune activation: CD8 T cells in proximity to pRPA32+ tumor cells exhibited significantly elevated GrzB expression. This indicates localized T cell activation neighbouring replication stress–high tumor cells, and provides evidence that the spatial proximity to distinct tumor phenotypes may prime or enhance cytotoxic immune responses. Altogether, our results are consistent with previous in vivo findings, supporting the ATR inhibitor-immunotherapy combination as a promising potential treatment option (36), highlighting the value of our fully humanized iPDC platform in uncovering novel combinatorial treatments and tissue-based predictive biomarkers in HGSC The spatial analyses show the potential of biomarker discovery combined with functional testing in iPDCs, however we acknowledge that the conclusions will require prospective clinical validation. .

vector_chunks.text:
ents and tissue-based predictive biomarkers in HGSC The spatial analyses show the potential of biomarker discovery combined with functional testing in iPDCs, however we acknowledge that the conclusions will require prospective clinical validation. .
The limitations of iPDCs include firstly their short-term culture duration, lasting up to one week, without the ability for passage over time. This constraint is inherent to short-term ex vivo models that aim to sustain the viability and functional states of the immune cells. Moreover, we focused our analyses on the most abundant immune cell populations, leaving room for minor cell populations to be further explored. Secondly, this platform only allows for the investigation of immunotherapy effects on intra-tumoral immune cells, but does not capture the mobility and priming of peripheral blood immune cells. Thirdly, the limited number of PARPi and chemoresistant patients in our study is a result of the difficulty in obtaining these samples, necessitating further validation with a larger cohort of recurrent samples. Despite these limitations, our iPDC platform harboring patient-matched intratumoral immune cells present a valuable preclinical model for evaluating the effectiveness and biomarkers for new targeted and immuno-oncology agents in HGSC (49).
DECLARATIONS
ETHICS APPROVAL AND CONSENT TO PARTICIPATE
All experiments involving patient materials were performed in accordance with the ethical standards from the 1975 Declaration of Helsinki. HGSC tissue samples were collected as a part of Oncosys-OVA study (clinical trial number: NCT06117384) at Helsinki University Hospital (HUH).

ent materials were performed in accordance with the ethical standards from the 1975 Declaration of Helsinki. HGSC tissue samples were collected as a part of Oncosys-OVA study (clinical trial number: NCT06117384) at Helsinki University Hospital (HUH). Each patient gave written informed consent, and the use of fresh tissue, blood, and formalin-fixed paraffin embedded (FFPE) HGSC clinical material was approved by the Ethics Committee of Women’s Clinic, HUH and Helsinki Biobank.
CONSENT FOR PUBLICATION
The consent form mentioned in the section above also covers consent for publication. This written informed consent form was obtained from all participants of the Oncosys-OVA study, including the patients whose tissue samples and clinical follow-up data were used in this manuscript. 
COMPETING INTERESTS
Authors declare that they have no competing interests.
FUNDING
This study was co-funded by The European Union (ERC, SPACE 101076096), Sigrid Jusélius Foundation, Cancer Society of Finland, Research council of Finland (grant numbers 1339805, 350396, 351196), and iCAN Digital Precision Cancer Medicine.

Reviewer comments and point-by-point responses
We thank the reviewers for their important insights on our work. We have now revised the manuscript and we have addressed all the comments, including an extensive restructuring of the figures, inclusion of supplemental data to the main figures, and edited numerous sections of the main text to improve the presentation of the data and to clarify the messages conveyed by our work.
The major changes to the work are:
Restructuring of the figures to greatly improve the display of main figures, and improved formatting of figures for better data visibility.
Inclusion of several panels of supplemental data into main figures to clarify presentation of results.
Inclusion of supporting information from prior murine model studies, and clinical trial data, answering the comments of the reviewers on justification of target selection and data set sizes. 
Revised the main text to ensure improved integration of our manuscript findings to the existing literature. 
Main novelties and impact of our study:
Humanized ex vivo model that recapitulates immune cell composition of source tumors, and can be used for preclinical testing of immunotherapy compounds on samples of both treatment-naïve and recurrence stages.
Display of discrete iPDC immune cell activation responses upon immunotherapy, and of cancer cell pathway activation in iPDCs in relation to clinical response status
Identification of replication stress biomarker, and spatial analysis on mechanics underlying ATRi response through effects on immune cell activation, confirming findings of previous studies and clinical trials.
Reviewer #1 (Comments to the Author ):
This paper by Nagaraj et al.

n stress biomarker, and spatial analysis on mechanics underlying ATRi response through effects on immune cell activation, confirming findings of previous studies and clinical trials.
Reviewer #1 (Comments to the Author ):
This paper by Nagaraj et al. is valuable in reporting that iPDCs using OmGel as an extracellular matrix can culture not only cancer cells but also immune cells ex vivo for 4-7 days, and can assess the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors.
However, although various experiments have been conducted, the results are descriptive, and the verification of the final conclusion that cancers positive for pRPA32 in cancer cells have PD-1 CD8-positive T cells in the tumor vicinity and show high efficacy of ATR inhibitors is insufficient.
Author response: Thank you for highlighting this point. We acknowledge that our sample number is a limitation of the study. Our hypothesis of replication stress stemmed from our previous translational work on a randomized phase 2 (RP2) study (NCI-CTEP #9944, NCT02595892) investigating the efficacy of an ATR inhibitor in ovarian cancer https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-25904-w). In the translational analyses, we found that genomic alterations associated with replication stress significantly associate with responses to ATRi. Thus, in line with these clinical trial results, our iPDC model confirms the association of replication stress with responses to ATRi, and the spatial analyses extend beyond the current knowledge, showing significant associations also to the spatial interactions with immune cells.
We have now added more details about the clinical trial results, and the reference to the above study as a rationale of investigating pRPA32 in the spatial studies (lines 433-434).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Files for Resubmission/Nagaraj_et_al_Rebuttal_Letter.docx::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Files for Resubmission/Nagaraj_et_al_Rebuttal_Letter.docx
vector_chunks.text:
cant associations also to the spatial interactions with immune cells.
We have now added more details about the clinical trial results, and the reference to the above study as a rationale of investigating pRPA32 in the spatial studies (lines 433-434). We have also edited the discussion to better convey that our results are in line with the clinical trial results, confirming the relevance of our preclinical iPDC model. We have also edited the discussion to fully acknowledge the limitations of our study (lines 485-487, 515-517).
The study demonstrates using iPDCs experiments that the efficacy of PD-1 inhibitors is high in HRD-positive cancers and low in HRP cancers. However, pRPA32, which is typically a replication stress marker, is higher in high-grade serous cancers with CCNE1 amplification (which accounts for 40% of HRP cancers) than in HRD ovarian cancers. 
Author response: We thank the reviewer for this important distinction between HR status and CCNE1 amplification.
The correlation between HR status and PD-1 inhibitor response has been previously presented using the samples of the TOPACIO trial (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-15315-8). Our study thus confirms the result using our novel iPDC model, while providing additional insights into the spatial correlates in the source tumors that can facilitate the development and further testing of clinical biomarkers. 
The use of pRPA32 expression as a predictor for ATRi response is not related to HR status or the PD-1 inhibition results, but as a biomarker for ATRi response as shown in the previous clinical trial translational work.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Files for Resubmission/Nagaraj_et_al_Rebuttal_Letter.docx::chunk_0003
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Files for Resubmission/Nagaraj_et_al_Rebuttal_Letter.docx
vector_chunks.text:
esting of clinical biomarkers. 
The use of pRPA32 expression as a predictor for ATRi response is not related to HR status or the PD-1 inhibition results, but as a biomarker for ATRi response as shown in the previous clinical trial translational work. In this translational work, we show that replication stress extends beyond CCNE1 amplification, and tumors commonly have other causes of replication stress (https://aacrjournals.org/cancerres/article/79/8/1730/640966/Exploiting-DNA-Replication-Stress-for-Cancer). In fact, in Konstantinopoulos et al, we showed that CCNE1 amplification accounts for only ~20 % of cases that exhibit replication stress, which correlated, also without CCNE1 amplification, with ATRi treatment response (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-25904-w). Thus, we did not want to limit ourselves to CCNE1 amplification status, but rather use a more functional downstream marker that indicates replication stress resulting from different processes. Furthermore, only two cases in this study had CCNE1 amplification, C238 and C053, both of which are HRP. C238 did not respond to ATRi, and had lower than average pRPA32 levels, whereas C053 was an ATRi responder with slightly higher than average levels of pRPA32. These data further indicate the importance of factors outside CCNE1 amplification status in assessing replication stress.
According to the reviewer’s comment, we have now added a display of HR status to the revised Figure 4 panels B-F to clarify the genomic profile of the patients. Additionally, we have clarified the in the manuscript text with the rationale for using pRPA32 as a predictor of ATRi response (lines 425-427).

vector_chunks.text:
a display of HR status to the revised Figure 4 panels B-F to clarify the genomic profile of the patients. Additionally, we have clarified the in the manuscript text with the rationale for using pRPA32 as a predictor of ATRi response (lines 425-427).
In the Figure 3 experiment, the relationship between pRPA32 and HRP is unclear and should be clarified, including the relationship with CCNE1 amplification or HRP. 
Author response: We thank the reviewer for this comment, and have improved the text and figures to better represent that pRPA32-mediated replication stress is not directly linked to HR status. The revised Figure 4 panel F has been updated to contain HR status, and further rationalization between pRPA32 expression and ATRi response has been added (lines 425-427).
Overall, the results are based on analyses of small sample sizes, with insufficient validation of validity, making interpretation of the results difficult.
Author response: We thank the reviewer for this comment, and we acknowledge the small sample sizes. The results on the improved responses of HRD tumors to immunotherapies are well aligned with our previous work on higher immunogenicity and PD-L1 expression in HRD/BRCAmut tumors (https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-022-28389-3), and also with the results from the TOPACIO Phase II clinical trial showing improved PFS in HRD patients when combining PARPi with Pembrolizumab in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer (https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-15315-8). Moreover, our results on the ATRi are in line with the Phase II clinical trial showing the efficacy of ATRi in ovarian cancer (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38635934/), which associate with a replication stress.

re.com/articles/s41467-020-15315-8). Moreover, our results on the ATRi are in line with the Phase II clinical trial showing the efficacy of ATRi in ovarian cancer (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38635934/), which associate with a replication stress. In addition, we have previously published comprehensive mechanistic characterization in vivo on the effects of targeting the LPA pathway (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35552618/). The fact that our results using our iPDC model faithfully recapitulate results obtained from clinical trials and mouse models highlight the high relevance of our fully humanized iPDC model in testing new therapeutic agents for HGSC. We thus believe that our work offers a proof of concept that iPDCs can be used for preclinical assessment of new therapeutic agents. We have now accordingly edited the manuscript text to better convey this message to the reader, and to more clearly indicate the limitations of this study (lines 477-478, 480-481).
Regarding the Figure 2 experiment, it has weak connections to Figures 1 and 3, is based on analysis of small sample sizes, and has low significance of results.
The finding that combination of ATR inhibitors and ATX inhibitors activates CD8 T cells and activates CD11c-positive myeloid cells is an important observation.
Author response: Thank you for this important remark. We have improved the coherence of the text between the sections (lines 344-346, 354, 367-368, 399). Additionally, we have made the proof-of-concept nature of our studies more obvious, and that previous studies have had similar results, which our study confirms (lines 365, 367-368, 403-404).

Reviewer #2 (Comments to the Author ):
This manuscript by Nagaraj et.al. presents a significant body of work (i) developing a novel patient derived organoid tumor model that incorporates an omental gel, (ii) validating the model, showing strong preservation of a diverse immune cell repertoire and correlating therapeutic response with patient response, (iii) using the platform to identify an ATR inhibitor/immunotherapy combination as a potential new therapeutic approach for ovarian cancer.
I view this paper as having many strengths and only a few weaknesses.
Major Concerns
1. PDC response to various therapies are tested (parp, ATRi etc), but response the primary therapy – carboplatin and taxol, is not. This would add to the paper to see the response correlation with patients response.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Files for Resubmission/Nagaraj_et_al_Rebuttal_Letter.docx::chunk_0007
vector_chunks.source_file: iPDC paper resubmission/iPDC new CyCIF results 2025/CIR revision/Files for Resubmission/Nagaraj_et_al_Rebuttal_Letter.docx
vector_chunks.text:
a few weaknesses.
Major Concerns
1. PDC response to various therapies are tested (parp, ATRi etc), but response the primary therapy – carboplatin and taxol, is not. This would add to the paper to see the response correlation with patients response.
Author response: We thank the reviewer for this important suggestion. However, we carefully considered different options for making this correlation in terms of the most accurate clinical variables. Firstly, correlation to primary therapy outcome (RECIST 1.1) would be impossible due to that during 1st line treatment, >90% of the patients achieve complete response to the combination of surgery and 1st line chemotherapy (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S235257892400119X). We also did not have serial measurements during chemotherapy to use blood biomarkers (e.g. Kelim score https://www.ejcancer.com/article/S0959-8049(23)00318-0/fulltext) to infer chemotherapy. PFS is also the most important response evaluation method recommended for 1st line ovarian cancer patients (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1048891X24038714). However, approximately 40-50% of HGSC patients receive PARPi as 1st line maintenance therapy, significantly confounding the analysis when making correlations of PFS. However, for the chemotherapy resistant patient whom we had access to recurrent disease sample, we investigated the responses to platinum-based chemotherapy in iPDCs and showed that the iPDCs were indeed resistant to platinum chemotherapy in line with the clinical response (Figure 3B).
Due to these limitations, we decided to associate the responses to PARP inhibitors, using time on PARPi and PFS as the clinical outcome measures in patients who have received PARP inhibitors as 1st line maintenance.

the clinical response (Figure 3B).
Due to these limitations, we decided to associate the responses to PARP inhibitors, using time on PARPi and PFS as the clinical outcome measures in patients who have received PARP inhibitors as 1st line maintenance. We show significant correlation between iPDC PARPi response and clinical responses of the patients correlate significantly (Figure 1G, H). We have now added a discussion about this rationale in the manuscript (lines 323-327). 
2. The PDC response to ATRi/ATXi is interesting and an important finding with clinical implications. It would be nice to see this validated in a murine model or humanized PDX model.
Author response: We agree on the relevance of our finding with ATRi/ATXi inhibitors in ovarian cancer. Importantly, the fact that our results are in line with the Phase II clinical trial showing the efficacy of ATRi in ovarian cancer (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38635934/) emphasizes the clinical relevance or our iPDC model. For the ATXi, we have previously published comprehensive mechanistic characterization in vivo on the effects of targeting the LPA pathway (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35552618/). Using genetically engineered, syngeneic mouse models of HGSC we show that ATXi reprogrammed the tumor immune microenvironment, extended host survival, and improved the effects of therapies that elicit protective responses driven by type I IFN. This again highlights the high relevance of our fully humanized iPDC model in testing new therapeutic agents for HGSC. We have now clarified and added clear references to the Chae et al to the manuscript (lines 406-409)
Minor –
1.

1. For the correlation of PDC response and duration response to therapy it is unclear how many patients were tested – it would be nice to see the individual data points to know if results are skewed by the one outlier.
Author response: We thank the reviewer for pointing out issues in our figure presentation. The individual data points in Figure 1 panel G were denoted in the original version of the panel, but we have increased their visibility, as well as for the trendline as well. Additionally, we have improved the visibility of the revised Figure 1 panel E trendline as well to ensure no data is lost from the figures. 
2. How long can the immune populations remain viable in this system – studies were from 4-7 days. It would be good to know how long viable immune cells are present.
Author response: We thank the reviewer for this comment. We have now clarified that the iPDC cell proportions depicted in Figure 2 panel A are obtained from experiment endpoints, and that the cells are viable, as the flow cytometry panel contains a viability dye (lines 728-730, Supplemental Figure 2 legend). To provide an additional intra-experiment data points of immune cell viability, we have added data on a new set of five patient samples and their immune cell proportion comparisons at day 0 and day 3/4, to further give an insight on immune cell proportion evolution during the iPDC experiments Supplemental Figure 2, panel F.

3. Would be nice to have some rationale for why ATRi and Wee1 inhibitors were chosen for the studies in figure 2 (there are lots of promising agents).
Author response: We thank the reviewer for raising this point. The treatments were based on our and others previous work investigating new treatment options for chemoresistant ovarian cancer. Previous clinical and preclinical work have suggested ATRi (Konstantinopoulos et al; https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-25904-w, Chae et al; https://doi.org/10.1158/2159-8290.Cd-21-1181) and Wee1 (Fu et al; https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.00830) as options for treatment for therapy resistant ovarian cancer. Additionally, our previous work displays that ATRi and WEE1i can be also effective upon PARPi resistance in vitro (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33514515/). We have added the rationalization to the manuscript text to increase the clarity of this rationale (lines 406-409).
4. Fig S4 A is important information but the label for the drugs used is not readable/text is too small.
Author response: Thank you for pointing out this issue. We have completely reformatted Supplemental Figure 4 panel A to improve the visibility of the labels.
5. Line 363 – reports patient responded for ‘xx’ months – this should be corrected.
Author response: Thank you for bringing this to our attention, we have now added the months as their correct numbers
6. C218_L is not explained anywhere in the text

7. In the discussion the paper mentions response to PERKi being tested, but this is not mentioned in the main results section. It is in the list of drugs tested and is in supplemental information. But if it is important enough to mention in the discussion it likely should be in the main paper.
Author response: We thank the reviewer for bringing this item for discussion, and have decided to remove discussion references to PERKi, as the response to this inhibitor is not significant enough to warrant singling it out amidst the high-throughput drug testing panel.
8. Im guessing this is an article formatting issue, but there is a lot of data jammed in just 3 figures. If room allows this would be better expanded into more figures and potentially including some of the supplemental information
Author response: We thank the reviewer for this important suggestion. We have now expanded the main figure count from three figures to five, and brought a total of five supplemental figure panels to the main figures. This has improved the flow and spacing of the figures, and created a better rhythm for the study.
Senior Editor Comments:
The novel human ex vivo experimental system presented is timely and of interest. Results provide proof-of-principle for the potential utility of this system to investigate combinations of targeted agents with immunotherapy.

Ex Vivo Immuno-Oncology Platform Reveals Spatial T Cell Infiltration Patterns Linked to ATR Inhibition Responses in High-Grade Serous Ovarian Cancer
Ashwini S Nagaraj1, Matilda Salko1†, Aditi Sirsikar1†, Ziqi Kang1, Erdogan Pekcan Erkan1,2, Elina A Pietilä1, Iga Niemiec1, Jie Bao1, Giovanni Marchi1, Angéla Szabó1, María Hincapié-Otero1, Anastasia Lundgren1, Kirsten Nowlan3, Sanna Pikkusaari1, Anna Kanerva4, Johanna Tapper4, Riitta Koivisto-Korander4, Heini Lassus1,4, Liisa Kauppi1, Sampsa Hautaniemi1, Anna Vähärautio1,5, Jing Tang1, Eliisa Kekäläinen3,6, Ulla-Maija Haltia1,4, Anni Virtanen1,7, Joonas Jukonen1, Tuula Salo8,9,10,11, Anniina Färkkilä1,12,13,14*

Helsinki, Helsinki, Finland.
